entry.title: Work Smarter with AI-Assisted Tasks in the SAP Activate Project Management Workstream
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series highlighting AI capabilities in SAP Activate. For additional information, visit the main blog here. 

Project Managers are expected to balance strategic planning with day-to-day execution while keeping projects on schedule, within budget, and aligned with business objectives. To help streamline these activities, the SAP Activate road map viewer now includes AI-assisted procedures for tasks within the Project Management workstream.

These AI-Assisted procedures are designed to help project managers quickly create high-quality project deliverables by combining SAP Activate best practices with generative AI. Rather than starting from a blank page, project teams can leverage guided AI prompts to accelerate planning, improve consistency, and reduce manual effort.

What Are AI-Assisted Procedures?

AI-Assisted procedures provide step-by-step guidance for using generative AI to complete common project management activities. Each procedure includes a structured prompt that incorporates SAP Activate methodology and best practices, helping generate a tailored starting point that can be reviewed and refined for your specific project.

The generated content is intended to support project teams by providing a solid first draft while allowing project managers to apply their expertise and project-specific knowledge before finalizing the deliverable.

The following Project Management tasks now include AI-Assisted procedures with the SAP Activate road map viewer:

Develop a Project Charter

A well-defined project charter establishes the project’s purpose, objectives, scope, governance, and success criteria. The AI-Assisted procedure helps generate a comprehensive project charter by incorporating information such as project goals, stakeholders, timelines, assumptions, constraints, risks, and governance structure.

This provides project managers with a structured draft that can be customized to meet the organization's specific requirements.

Estimate Backlog Items

Accurate backlog estimation is essential for planning releases and managing project delivery. The AI-Assisted procedure assists teams in developing consistent effort estimates by analyzing backlog item descriptions and applying estimation guidance based on SAP Activate best practices.

The resulting estimates can be reviewed and refined by the project team during backlog and sprint planning activities.

Develop a Hypercare Support Plan

Preparing for a post-go-live support is critical to ensuring a successful transition into operations. The AI-Assisted procedure helps generate a structured Hypercare Support plan that outlines support objectives, roles and responsibilities, issue management processes, communication plans, monitoring activities, escalation paths, and exit criteria.

This provides implementation teams with a comprehensive starting point for planning the Hypercare period.

Handover Operations Responsibility

A smooth transition from the implementation project to the operational support organization is key to long-term success. The AI-Assisted procedure assists project teams in developing an operational handover plan that defines transition activities, knowledge transfer, documentation requirements, support responsibilities, and operational readiness.

The generated plan helps ensure that operational teams receive the information they need to effectively support the solution after go-live.

Communicate Project Status and Progress

Keeping stakeholders informed with timely, accurate, and consistent project updates is essential for effective project governance. The AI-Assisted procedure helps project managers create a comprehensive project status report by generating a structured summary of overall project health, milestone progress, schedule and budget status, key accomplishments, risks and issues, upcoming activities, and decisions requiring stakeholder attention.

This provides project managers with a professional, SAP Activate-aligned starting point that can be reviewed, tailored with project specific information, and shared with stakeholders to support informed decision making and transparent project communication.

Why Use AI-Assisted Procedures?

By incorporating AI-Assisted procedures into the SAP Activate methodology project teams can:

  • Accelerate the creation of common project management deliverables.
  • Start with structured, SAP Activate-aligned content instead of a blank document.
  • Improve consistency across projects by leveraging standardized guidance.
  • Reduce the time spent drafting project documentation.
  • Allow project managers to focus more on project leadership, stakeholder engagement, and decision-making.

These procedures are designed to enhance, not replace, the expertise of experienced project managers. Human review remains an essential part of the process to validate accuracy, tailor the content to the project, and ensure alignment with organizational standards.

The AI-Assisted Procedure tag helps you quickly identify tasks that include guidance for using generative AI. These tasks contain an AI-Assisted procedure and a link to the SAP Activate Prompt Library, making it easy to generate first drafts of project deliverables while following SAP Activate best practices.

entry.title: AI-Assisted Extensibility Planning & Design in SAP Activate for SAP S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series that explains how to leverage SAP Business AI in your implementation projects. For additional context, please refer to the main blog post, SAP Business AI in SAP Activate: Three Dimensions of AI for Your Implementation

Clean Core and Extensibility in SAP Activate

SAP Activate for SAP Cloud ERP Private includes a dedicated Clean Core Success Plan that spans all project phases. The methodology establishes clean core governance early. A Solution Standardization Board is set up during Prepare, extensibility scenarios are evaluated and classified before detailed design begins, and Clean Core Quality Gates run through the project to track progress against clean core objectives.

For extensibility, SAP's clean core approach distinguishes between two architectural patterns. On-stack extensibility covers extensions that run directly on SAP S/4HANA and require tight coupling with core data, transactions, or applications. Side-by-side extensibility on SAP BTP covers loosely coupled extensions that interact with the core through released remote APIs and events. When planning extensions, SAP recommends starting with what SAP BTP can offer, as this supports upgrade readiness and innovation potential. Where business or technical requirements clearly call for proximity to the core, on-stack extensions remain the right choice.

On-stack extensions should be built using the ABAP Cloud development model, which enforces clean core compliance through compiler checks and restricts usage to publicly released SAP APIs and extension points. Classic extensibility (custom ABAP development using SAP internal objects or modifications) is still available in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and on-premise, but carries higher upgrade risk and sits at the lower end of the clean core level scale. The goal is to reach Level A extensions built with SAP Build using only publicly released, stable interfaces, and to eliminate Level D extensions that use non-recommended objects, modifications, or direct write access to SAP tables.

The extensibility workstream in the Explore phase is where most of the documentation work happens: consolidating requirements from fit-to-standard workshops, producing functional specifications for each RICEFW object, and then moving into detailed technical design for those that need custom development. These are necessary steps, but they are time-consuming.

AI-Assisted Procedures in SAP Activate

SAP Activate now includes AI-Assisted Procedures for selected tasks across the SAP Cloud ERP Private - New Implementation roadmap. Each one is embedded directly in the task description in the SAP Roadmap Viewer and connects to a specific prompt in the SAP Activate Prompt Library, which is hosted within the Roadmap Viewer.

Tasks with an AI-Assisted Procedure are tagged in the SAP Roadmap Viewer with two tags: SAP Business AI and AI-Assisted Procedure. These tags can be used to filter the roadmap and quickly identify all tasks that carry AI-assisted guidance, without navigating through the full task list.

For the extensibility workstream, three of these AI-Assisted Procedures support the documentation and design effort leading up to development.

 

SAP Joule for Consultants

SAP Joule for Consultants is the SAP Business AI capability used to execute the prompts discussed in this blog. It is the SAP Consulting capability within Joule, available to consultants working on SAP engagements, that provides access to expert templates and project-specific prompt execution. Consultants provide their project inputs, attach relevant documents where required, and SAP Joule for Consultants generates a structured first-draft deliverable ready for review and refinement.

The AI-Assisted Procedures in this blog all target SAP Joule for Consultants. SAP Activate is expanding the scope of SAP Business AI across the methodology, and future procedures will leverage additional Joule and SAP Business AI capabilities beyond the consulting capability described here. For more information, see the SAP Joule for Consultants Service Guide.

Using the SAP Activate Prompt Library

The SAP Activate Prompt Library is accessible directly from the SAP Roadmap Viewer. Each prompt entry is structured to give consultants everything they need to run the prompt and understand the expected output before they start. Each entry includes the following fields:

Field Description

Prompt NameThe name of the prompt, which corresponds to the deliverable it produces.
RoadmapThe SAP Activate roadmap or roadmaps the prompt applies to.
TaskThe specific task in the roadmap where the prompt is used.
Target JouleThe Joule capability used to execute the prompt.
Expert TemplateAn optional expert template that structures the Joule session for the specific use case.
Attachments / RequirementsThe documents required or recommended as input when running the prompt.
Sample OutputA worked example showing the format and depth of the deliverable the prompt produces.

The prompt library is the starting point before running any AI-Assisted Procedure. Reviewing the sample output for the relevant prompt gives consultants a clear picture of what SAP Joule for Consultants will produce and what inputs are needed.

 

Extensibility Design and Planning Prompts within SAP Activate

Document Extensibility Requirements

The "Document Extensibility Requirements" task in the Explore phase is where all extensibility needs identified during fit-to-standard workshops get formally documented, classified, and signed off by the Solution Standardization Board before design begins. Producing a complete Functional Specification for each RICEFW object (covering the gap justification, process flows, authorization requirements, data model considerations, and test conditions) is the core output of this task and typically requires significant consultant time per object.

SAP Activate Task: Document Extensibility Requirements
SAP Joule for Consultants Prompt: Functional Specification Document

Takes the RICEFW object list from the workshops as its primary input, with the SAP Activate Lean Specifications Template as an optional attachment. Produces a complete, structured FSD for each object covering gap analysis, functional description, authorization matrix, process flows, data model, dependencies, and test scenarios in a format directly usable in MS Word.

Detail Design of Extension with Classic Extensibility Techniques

Once requirements are documented and clean-core alternatives are confirmed as insufficient, the detailed technical design for each classic extension needs to be produced. This requires evaluating the extension against SAP's Clean Core Extensibility framework and producing a specification precise enough for development: entry points, APIs or extension technologies, enhancement logic, data model changes, and authorizations.

SAP Activate Task: Detail Design of Extension with Classic Extensibility Techniques
SAP Joule for Consultants Prompt: Design Analysis and Technical Specification

Takes the Functional Specification document from the previous task as its input. For each RICEFW object, it determines the clean core compliance level with justification, then generates the full technical design specification: entry point, released API or extension technology, enhancement logic and process flow, data model considerations, and authorization requirements. Where a cleaner alternative exists or should be evaluated in a future release, the output flags it.

These two prompts connect directly. The FSD produced by the first is the mandatory input for the second. The RICEFW object list from workshops flows through to a complete technical design specification with one human review step in between.

Development of Clean Core Compliant Extensions

Before development begins, all extension objects need consistent, project-specific technical names that comply with the agreed customer namespace, Clean Core tier, and SAP naming rules. Without this, naming decisions get made ad hoc by individual developers, leading to inconsistencies across workstreams that are difficult to correct once objects exist in the system.

SAP Activate Task: Development of Clean Core Compliant Extensions
SAP Joule for Consultants Prompt: Extensibility Naming Conventions

Takes the project RICEFW List, a sample Functional Specification Document header, and the project Extensibility Blueprint as inputs. Produces a complete, project-ready Extensibility Naming Convention document covering all three Clean Core extensibility tiers: Developer, Key User, and Side-by-Side, with naming patterns, length constraints, examples, and counter-examples for every object type. Includes a Complete Object Naming Register that validates every object from the RICEFW List against the agreed conventions and flags deviations.

All three prompts connect as a chain. The RICEFW object list from workshops flows through to a complete technical design specification and a validated naming convention document, with one human review step between each stage.

Step 1: Document Extensibility Requirements
Functional Specification Document prompt → FSD per RICEFW object

FSD output becomes mandatory input for Step 2

Step 2: Detail Design of Extension with Classic Extensibility Techniques
Design Analysis and Technical Specification prompt → Clean Core assessment + Technical Design Spec

Technical Design Spec and RICEFW List become inputs for Step 3

Step 3: Development of Clean Core Compliant Extensions
Extensibility Naming Conventions prompt → Project-ready Naming Convention document + Complete Object Naming Register

All three prompts are available in the SAP Activate Prompt Library.

What's Next

The SAP Activate team is actively identifying more ways to leverage SAP Business AI within the SAP Activate methodology to accelerate the implementation of SAP solutions. The extensibility prompts are part of a broader effort to embed AI assistance at the points in a project where documentation and decision-making effort is highest. With the addition of the Extensibility Naming Conventions prompt, the AI-assisted extensibility coverage now extends into the Realize phase, supporting naming governance before development begins.

All three prompts are accessible in the SAP Activate Prompt Library within the SAP Roadmap Viewer: Functional Specification Document, Design Analysis and Technical Specification, and Extensibility Naming Conventions.

Related Resources

  • SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer
  • SAP Joule for Consultants Service Guide
  • SAP Cloud ALM for Implementation - Expert Portal
  • Clean Core Extensibility for SAP S/4HANA Cloud

entry.title: Transform Your Fit-to-Standard Workshop Preparation with AI-Assisted Procedures
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series highlighting AI capabilities in SAP Activate. For an overview of the complete series, visit the main blog post here: SAP Business AI in SAP Activate: Three Dimensions ... - SAP Community

What if AI could help your project team prepare Fit-to-Standard workshops faster, more consistently, and with greater confidence?

Now it can.

Why Fit-to-Standard preparation matters

Preparing for Fit-to-Standard workshops is one of the most important activities in an SAP implementation.

Before the workshops begin, the project team must:

  • Understand and validate the implementation scope.
  • Map the business processes and solutions to be demonstrated.
  • Identify the appropriate stakeholders.
  • Define clear roles and responsibilities.
  • Prepare a detailed workshop agenda.
  • Determine the required inputs and expected outcomes.

This preparation is essential—but it can also require significant time and coordination.

What if an AI-assisted procedure could help the project team perform these activities?

We have exciting news.

AI assistance is now embedded in SAP Activate

SAP Activate now includes AI-Assisted Procedures for selected tasks within the Application: Design and Configuration workstream of the SAP Cloud ERP Private – New Implementation roadmap.

These procedures combine SAP Activate best practices with generative AI to help project teams create comprehensive Fit-to-Standard workshop preparation and planning materials more efficiently.

Each AI-Assisted Procedure is:

  • Embedded directly in the task description in SAP Roadmap Viewer.
  • Connected to a task-specific prompt.
  • Supported by the SAP Activate Prompt Library.
  • Designed to fit naturally into the implementation workflow.

This means project teams can access AI guidance precisely where they need it—without leaving SAP Roadmap Viewer.

Let’s explore “Prepare for Fit-to-Standard Workshops”

Open the Prepare for Fit-to-Standard Workshops task in SAP Roadmap Viewer.

Inside the task, you will find an AI-Assisted Procedure that guides you through the workshop preparation process. Open the procedure and follow steps 1 through 9.

The procedure helps you organize the necessary project information and use the corresponding prompt to generate a structured Fit-to-Standard workshop preparation plan.

Two new accelerators to support the process

We have also created two accelerators to help project teams use the procedure and its prompt more effectively:

  1. Fit-to-Standard Workshop Notes — a structured template for capturing the information required to prepare and run the prompt.
  2. SAP Activate Prompt Library — a collection of prompts supporting multiple activities across the SAP Activate roadmaps, including the Prepare for Fit-to-Standard Workshops prompt.

The SAP Activate Prompt Library is available directly from the SAP Roadmap Viewer. Simply click the prompt referenced in the task to access it.

Each prompt entry provides consultants with the information they need to:

  • Understand the purpose of the prompt.
  • Identify the required inputs.
  • Execute the prompt correctly.
  • Review the expected output before getting started.

A structured prompt based on the COP Framework

The prompts follow the COP Prompt Framework, which is organized around:

  • Context — the project background and information the AI needs.
  • Objectives — the results the prompt should produce.
  • Preferences — the expected structure, level of detail, and output format.

This framework makes the prompts easier to understand, execute, and adapt to the specific needs of each project.

What does the prompt generate?

The Fit-to-Standard Workshop Preparation Plan prompt can generate:

  1. Scope Validation Checklist — a pre-workshop sign-off checklist for the System Integrator Lead and Customer Project Manager.
  2. Business Process Scoping Matrix — maps in-scope functional areas to SAP Best Practices scope item IDs, workshop dates, and business owners.
  3. Business Stakeholder Pre-Work — an actionable checklist of items due at least five business days before each workshop.
  4. System Readiness Checklist — covers sandbox provisioning, SAP Best Practices activation, master data, user access, and SAP Cloud ALM setup.
  5. Workshop Agenda Templates — includes a general full-day Fit-to-Standard agenda and tailored versions for functional areas such as Finance, Procure-to-Pay, Order-to-Cash, Manufacturing, Warehouse Management, and Asset Management.
  6. Fit-to-Standard Tracking Template — provides a detailed gap log, including one row for each delta requirement identified in the workshop notes.
  7. Roles and RACI Matrix — defines accountability across key customer, system integrator, architecture, consulting, and project management roles.
  8. Gap Capture Guidance for SAP Cloud ALM — provides a step-by-step process for recording gaps, completing mandatory attributes, assigning resolution categories, and maintaining traceability.
  9. Risks and Mitigation Strategies — creates a risk register covering workshop execution, scope, stakeholders, and technical readiness.
  10. Classification Decision Tree — supports consistent classification of requirements as Fit, Partial Fit, Gap, Out of Scope, or Deferred.

A representative example is also available in the Sample Output section of the prompt library.

What are the benefits?

AI-Assisted Procedures can help project teams:

  • Accelerate Fit-to-Standard workshop preparation.
  • Generate agendas based on the project scope.
  • Improve visibility into required resources, stakeholders, and inputs.
  • Reduce the time needed to validate workshop topics and schedules.
  • Establish a structured timetable for the topics to be discussed.
  • Apply SAP Activate guidance more consistently.
  • Start from a comprehensive, structured plan that the project team can review and refine.

AI supports the preparation—but the project team remains responsible for reviewing, validating, and adapting the output to the project context.

This is just the beginning

AI-Assisted Procedures bring SAP Activate guidance and generative AI together directly within the implementation workflow.

And this is only the beginning—more AI-assisted content is on the way.

Are you ready to use the Fit-to-Standard Workshop Preparation prompt in your next project?

Try it and share your experience in the comments. We would love to learn how you used the prompt, what value it brought to your project, and which SAP Activate task you would like to see supported next.

entry.title: What's New in SAP Business AI for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608
entry.content:
The SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 release marks another important step in SAP's journey toward the Autonomous Enterprise. As SAP Business AI continues to evolve, it's no longer just about automating individual tasks. Instead, AI is becoming a trusted collaborator, handling repetitive work, surfacing the right insights, and coordinating increasingly complex business processes.

Think of a modern production line. Every station has its own responsibility, but the real efficiency comes from how smoothly everything works together. SAP Business AI plays a similar role across the enterprise, helping coordinate tasks, surface the right information at the right time, and keep business processes moving without interrupting the people who drive them.

The 2608 release brings this vision to life through innovations across multiple dimensions of enterprise AI. In this blog, we'll explore a selection of highlights spanning Agentic AI, the latest Joule innovations, and embedded AI capabilities across SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. From intelligent finance agents and AI-powered workspaces to natural language experiences and embedded business process intelligence, these innovations are designed to make everyday work more intuitive, efficient, and context-aware.

Rather than introducing AI as a standalone capability, these innovations embed intelligence directly into the flow of work, helping finance teams accelerate period-end closing, simplifying compliance management, improving project visibility, and making interactions with SAP software feel more natural than ever before.

Take a look at the short video below to see how these AI-driven innovations are helping organizations work smarter today while bringing SAP's vision of the Autonomous Enterprise to life:

 

Agentic AI highlights

Finance

Receivables & Payables Clearing Agent (planned for 2608.2)

Matching open receivables and payables is often a repetitive and time-consuming task. Finance teams need to manually review invoices, identify matching items, and validate clearing proposals, making the process prone to delays and human error.

The new Receivables and Payables Clearing Agent uses SAP Business AI to analyze open items and generate balanced clearing proposals. The agent provides clear explanations for its matching recommendations, allowing accountants to quickly review, validate, and approve proposals before they are automatically posted as clearing documents.

By automating invoice matching and providing transparent AI recommendations, the Clearing Agent helps finance teams accelerate period-end closing, reduce manual effort, and improve operational efficiency—allowing accountants to spend less time on repetitive reconciliation tasks and more time on value-added financial analysis.

 

 

Journal entry agent (planned for 2608.3)

Creating journal entries is an essential part of every finance team's work, but preparing them from spreadsheet data often involves repetitive manual steps. Mapping fields, validating information, and creating journal entries can take valuable time, especially during busy period-end closing activities.

The new Journal Entry Agent makes this process faster and easier. Simply upload your spreadsheet through Joule, and let the agent analyze the data, map it to the required journal entry fields, and generate journal entry proposals automatically. From there, finance teams can review, adjust, and approve the proposals in the Manage Journal Entry Proposals app before posting them.

By reducing manual effort while keeping users in full control, the Journal Entry Agent helps improve accuracy, accelerate period-end closing, and free finance professionals to spend less time on routine data preparation and more time on delivering valuable financial insights.

 

 

Joule innovation highlights

Joule for Business: Moving to SAP-managed

Getting started with AI should be simple. Today, customers need to activate and configure Joule themselves by setting up the required services, identities, roles, and integrations. While this provides flexibility, it also adds complexity before users can start benefiting from SAP Business AI.

Now, Joule is transitioning from a customer-managed to a SAP-managed experience. Instead of handling the technical setup themselves, customers will simply access Joule through SAP for Me, while SAP takes care of the underlying configuration and lifecycle management. This approach also enables SAP to deliver new Joule innovations and enhancements more quickly, ensuring customers can benefit from the latest AI capabilities as they become available.

By removing setup complexity, SAP-managed Joule allows customers to focus on using AI rather than configuring it. For existing customers, a lightweight migration path is planned, with additional details and timelines to be shared in the future. Customers currently implementing Joule can continue with the existing customer-managed setup and seamlessly migrate when SAP-managed Joule becomes available.

 

 

 

Joule for Consultants

Expert Workspace

As organizations increasingly rely on AI for different business scenarios, keeping AI assistants organized and easy to manage becomes just as important as creating them. Without a structured workspace, it can be difficult to find the right AI expert or maintain specialized knowledge across projects and teams.

Expert Workspace in Joule introduces a more streamlined way to organize and manage AI Experts. Each Expert can be tailored with its own instructions and reference files, enabling more focused, context-aware conversations for specific business scenarios. New search, tagging, and filtering capabilities make it easy to quickly find the right Expert, while improved visibility into attachment storage helps users manage their growing AI workspaces more effectively.

By making AI Experts easier to organize, discover, and reuse, Expert Workspace helps users build specialized AI assistants for different workflows, improving productivity while creating a more scalable and efficient AI experience across the enterprise. 

 

 

Joule Work

As AI becomes part of everyday business, users need more than a chatbot, they need an intelligent workspace that brings together insights, automates routine work, and helps them take action. Switching between applications and manually coordinating tasks slows decision-making and reduces productivity.

With the release 2608, Joule Work introduces a dynamic AI workspace that goes beyond chat. Powered by user intent and business context, Joule Work surfaces the right insights, coordinates AI agents and assistants, and helps users complete business tasks from a single place. Whether asking questions in natural language, working in AI-generated Spaces, or accessing AI-powered workflows across desktop and mobile, users can move seamlessly from insight to action.

By bringing conversations, AI-powered workspaces, and intelligent automation together in one experience, Joule Work helps users make faster decisions, reduce context switching, and focus on the work that drives business value: another important step toward SAP's vision of the Autonomous Enterprise.

 

 

Embedded AI highlights

User Experience

AI-assisted easy fill of SAP Fiori elements-based applications

One of the highlights of this release is the general availability of AI-assisted easy fill.

Business users often receive information through emails, meeting notes, chat messages, or phone conversations. Traditionally, they then have to transfer this information manually into ERP transactions—finding the correct fields, switching between screens, and ensuring every value is entered correctly. AI-assisted easy fill removes much of this manual effort.

By simply describing a business update in natural language, SAP Business AI automatically interprets the information and populates the appropriate fields within the business object. Users can then review the suggested values before confirming them, ensuring both speed and accuracy.

This not only reduces repetitive data entry, but also helps improve data quality, lowers the learning curve for new users, and allows experienced users to complete everyday business processes significantly faster.

 

AI-assisted easy filter

We're also expanding natural language capabilities with AI-assisted easy filter. Filtering business data has traditionally required users to know exactly which filter fields exist and how they should be combined. Now, instead of manually building complex filter criteria, users can simply describe what they're looking for in plain language.

SAP Business AI understands the request and automatically translates it into the appropriate filters, including complex combinations such as amounts, currencies, dates, sorting, and grouping. Recent queries and favorite searches are also available, making frequently used searches even faster.

The result is a more intuitive search experience that helps both occasional and experienced users find business information with less effort and greater confidence.

 

Environment, Health, and Safety

AI-assisted compliance management

Managing compliance obligations often means reviewing lengthy regulations, permits, or corporate policies and manually translating them into operational tasks. It's a time-consuming process that can increase the risk of errors or overlooked obligations.

With the 2608 release, AI-assisted compliance management helps automate this work. Users can upload compliance documents as PDFs, and SAP Business AI automatically identifies relevant requirements, proposes structured compliance obligations, and recommends the corresponding operational tasks—such as periodic inspections, maintenance notifications, or reporting activities. A side-by-side view of the original document and the AI-generated suggestions allows users to quickly validate every recommendation while remaining in full control.

By reducing manual data entry and accelerating the setup of compliance obligations, AI-assisted compliance management helps organizations improve accuracy, reduce compliance risks, and stay audit-ready. Instead of spending time extracting information from lengthy documents, compliance professionals can focus on managing risks and ensuring regulatory compliance.

 

Professional Services

AI-assisted aging work in progress analytics and management

For project-based businesses, keeping track of aging work in progress (WIP) is essential for maintaining healthy cash flow. But identifying overdue WIP, understanding its root causes, and deciding what action to take often requires time-consuming analysis across multiple reports.

AI-assisted aging work in progress analytics and management helps project financial controllers gain these insights faster. Using Joule, users can analyze and visualize WIP aging across defined time buckets, while AI automatically generates executive summaries that highlight critical items and recommend the next steps. Intelligent drill-down navigation and automated billing notifications make it easier to investigate issues and accelerate resolution.

By transforming complex project data into actionable insights, this innovation helps finance and project teams reduce manual analysis, speed up billing cycles, lower cash flow risks, and spend more time making informed decisions instead of creating reports.

 

 

Summary 

🎦Register for our SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 Early Release Series! 

Between July 13-17, we are hosting a series of 24 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 release. On our landing page you can register for the entire series or pick the sessions most relevant to you. 

 

🔎Find all our assets  

The release 2608 showcases more than 130 innovation highlights across the portfolio. Check out our release document for all the details!

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: Joule Meets Classic UI
entry.content:
Joule meets Classic UI — bringing AI into SAP GUI for Windows and SAP Business Client

Joule meets Classic UI

 SAP Business Client integrates the Joule available in the Fiori Launchpad with native SAP GUI for Windows transactions. When integration is active, an extra window hosts Joule.

Joule can access the current application context and Frontend Actions so it can interact with the open transaction and perform context-specific tasks.

How to use Joule for SAP GUI for Windows transactions
1. Enable the Joule integration via the setting <EnableJouleInSapgui> to true in the <SingleOptions>-segment of the Administrator configuration file, see Administrator Configuration.
2. Define the Edge browser-setting <TrackingPreventionLevel> to Basic or None in the <EdgeSettings>-segment of the Administrator configuration file, see Administrator Configuration.
3. Start SAP Business Client and connect to an SAP Fiori Launchpad connection where Joule has been made available.
4. Open a native SAP GUI for Windows transaction inside SAP Business Client.
5. If a valid Joule instance was located within the Fiori Launchpad environment, a Joule button appears in the top area of the SAP GUI for Windows Header Toolbar. Click the button to launch the Joule UI.
6. Use Joule capabilities mapped to your scenario. Joule can take the current application context and invoke Frontend Actions to perform tasks in the open transaction.

 

Figure 1: Press Joule button

Figure 1: Press Joule button

Figure 2: Joule UI available

Figure 2: Joule UI available

Available Frontend Actions

·     DescribeUI: Access the UI tree of the current SAP GUI screen (useful to locate controls/elements).

·     ExecuteScript: Run an SAP GUI script silently (no user prompts).

·     ExecuteGuidedScript: Run an SAP GUI script with user prompts and UI highlighting.

Important: Frontend Actions must be exposed via Joule capabilities and mapped to the scenarios/use-cases you intend to automate or assist.

Demo-Videos are available.

See also: SAP-samples in github repository

entry.title: The SAP Change Makers Podcast: All Episodes in One Place
entry.content:
The SAP Change Makers Podcast: All Episodes in One Place

Your always-up-to-date guide to every conversation — newest episode first. Bookmark this page!

Foreword

When we launched the SAP Change Makers Podcast in the summer of 2021, we had a simple idea: give a microphone to the people who actually drive change — the leaders, experts, and practitioners inside and around SAP who turn big ideas into real outcomes for our customers.

Since then, we've explored quantum computing before it was on anyone's roadmap. We've gone behind the scenes of the SAP Mission Control Center — "the best kept secret in the industry." We've talked resilience with the teams that keep Black Friday running, and most recently, the rise of Agentic AI that is redefining what customer support even means.

What connects all these conversations? Every guest is a change maker: someone who plants an idea, drives a vision forward, and executes. And every episode gives you something you can use — in 10 to 30 minutes, on your commute, at your desk, or wherever you listen.

This article is the permanent home of the complete episode collection. Every time a new episode goes live, it appears right at the top of the list below. So bookmark this page, subscribe on your favorite platform, and check back — the newest conversation is always just one scroll away.

Let's dive in. 🎧

— Oliver Huschke, Host of the SAP Change Makers Podcast

🎧Subscribe & Listen

  • Show all episodes: SAP Change Makers on openSAP
  • Apple Podcasts: Subscribe
  • Spotify: Subscribe
  • TuneIn: Listen
  • Amazon Music: Listen

All Episodes — Newest First

🆕 AI is Transforming Support: Navigating the Autonomous Enterprise

entry.title: SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 1 in review
entry.content:
Having AI is not the same as using AI. Buying a solution is not the same as adopting it. That gap between what's deployed and what's actually running live, generating value day in and day out is exactly what SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week is built around.

Day 1 "Understand: The Enterprise AI Shift - From Vision to Action" set the foundation. Sessions covered the state of enterprise AI adoption, practical tools for building AI literacy at scale, a structured method for identifying high-value agentic AI use cases, and a candid look at what separates organizations generating real value from those still stuck in exploration mode. The day ended with a first look at Joule Work, SAP's new unified AI workspace.

Here's my recap of the day's sessions and the questions that came up most.

The opening keynote centered on SAP's vision for the Autonomous Enterprise: humans keep the decisions that require judgment, creativity, and accountability, while AI handles the coordination and execution. The strategy is set. The technology is ready. The gap is execution because AI adoption is hard work, and learning is what bridges it. From building AI literacy to hands-on product understanding, it is what enables individuals and teams to turn innovation into measurable business value.

What does it actually take to move an entire organization from AI-curious to AI-capable? It starts with AI literacy, the skills and learning that turn AI investment into real capability. With 86% of companies unable to build it fast enough, the pressure has never been higher. SAP's internal campaign, "Let's work the AI way," put the scale in perspective: 230+ AI use cases live, 500+ data products, and a value realization ambition exceeding €2Bn. The lesson from SAP's own experience: don't wait, and don't treat learning as a one-time event.

Your most asked questions:

  • What is NotebookLM and how do I access it?
    NotebookLM is an AI learning companion built into learning.sap.com. It lets you interact with course content conversationally rather than consuming it linearly, no separate activation needed, just a Learning Hub subscription. Partners report it cuts 20+ hour learning journeys down to around 5 hours.
  • What AI certifications are available and where do I start?
    Your starting point depends on your role and learning goals. Explore SAP’s AI learning portfolio on learning.sap.com/artificial-intelligence to find learning opportunities for different audiences. Business users can start with Introducing Joule and Getting Started with Joule for Business Users, while developers can work toward the SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer certification. Partners can also explore role-based learning paths and Certification Weeks through the SAP Partner Portal.
  • How much time should I invest, and does format matter?
    Two hours per week is a reasonable floor for foundational AI literacy. But format matters more than hours: 2–3 day on-site bootcamps and hackathons consistently outperform self-paced resources alone, based on SAP's own adoption data.

Want to learn more about building AI literacy at scale and what SAP has learned from its own adoption journey? Rewatch Opening Keynote: SAP AI Adoption and Learning Week and AI Literacy to Impact: Skills and Change Management, and How SAP Runs SAP.

Once the literacy conversation is underway, teams hit the next wall: which problems are actually worth solving with agentic AI and how do you identify them? Not every process needs an agent. The SAP AppHaus team introduced a practical, human-centered method for making that call: the Joule Agent Discovery Workshop,  a practical, human-centered method that helps a cross-functional team identify and prioritize a high-value agentic AI use case.  A follow-on Joule Agent Design Workshop turns that into a blueprint ready for configuration in Joule Studio. All workshop materials are freely available at apphaus.sap.com. Rewatch Discovering High-Value Opportunities for Agentic AI to see them in action.

Your most asked questions:

  • Should you use agents for deterministic use cases like finance or planning?
    Yes, but constrain how the LLM handles factual data. For precise answers, give the agent a tool (an API, a database call) and require it to return that result as-is. Use the LLM only for language, summarizing or formatting the output, not generating the answer. Grounding works when the agent is explicitly prevented from going beyond the retrieved data.
  • Is there a framework for rethinking processes for Agentic AI?
    The key shift is to stop designing every step and define the outcome instead: goal, constraints, and Agent reasoning. Agents find their own path. SAP’s integration of SAP Signavio with Agent Hub goes further by using process mining to observe how Agents behave and improve from there.

Frameworks point the way, but they don't tell you what it feels like to move an entire organization pilot mode to generating real AI value. A candid fireside chat with draw on direct experience with early adopters, three themes stood out: Focus beats breadth, AI adoption is also a team sport, and none of it scales without an executive sponsor.

And the bigger ambition? The organizations that lead won't just automate what exists. They'll reinvent it. Deep dive into the key themes and Rewatch the Fireside Chat: From Exploration to Enterprise Value - Lessons from Early SAP Business AI Adopters.

So what does reinventing work actually look like in practice?  In Joule Work, users express in natural language what they want to accomplish and a dynamic workspace that, based on people's intent, surfaces the right insights and automates routine work. Once a user starts a conversation, a Joule Assistant steps in: role-based AI that understands your context and responsibilities. Assistants coordinate teams of Joule Agents, which autonomously execute complex workflows across systems and business domains. The actions agents take are defined by Joule Skills, predefined capabilities like fetching data or triggering a workflow. Early Adopter Care is planned for July 16. General availability is planned for October 2026. Rewatch Meet Joule Work.

Your most asked questions:

  • What is Joule Work, and how is it different from other Joule products?
    Joule is embedded directly into SAP solutions like S/4HANA and SuccessFactors, contextual to whatever you are doing inside that system. Joule Work sits above that: it spans your entire SAP landscape and lets you act on end-to-end processes in one place, without switching applications. The two are connected and you can launch Joule Work directly from the embedded experience in full-screen mode.
  • Are Spaces permanent? Can I share them with colleagues?
    A space is a dynamic, AI-generated workspace for simple and complex problem-solving. Spaces are persistent. They are never automatically deleted, unless you want to, and can be reused across sessions. You can pin them for quick access, and they stay exactly as you left them. On sharing: collaboration is not yet available in the current Early Adopter phase.

Day 1 set the foundation. The rest of the week is about putting it all into practice. Continue with the full series:

  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 2 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 3 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 4 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 1 in review

entry.title: The Board Decides, the CIO Delivers: A Digital Health Platform Powered by SAP
entry.content:
In my previous posts I described SAP’s global healthcare industry strategy, a modular, data-driven, open, and interoperable platform, and I argued that a digital health platform (DHP) is ultimately a business decision that belongs on the CEO’s desk. Once the board has made that call, however, the mandate lies with the CIO, who turns a strategic ambition into a governed and sequenced delivery program that runs alongside daily operations without disrupting them. This post is written for that very role, and it tries to focus on two questions: what does a digital health platform powered by SAP actually consist of, and why does it make the CIO’s task easier instead of adding yet another program to an already full portfolio?

A landscape grown by change, not by design

In my experience, most healthcare providers definitely do not suffer from a lack of applications; they suffer from a system landscape that has grown over many years and rarely by design, and the figures show this: a symplr survey of health system clinicians and CIOs found that a majority of organizations run somewhere between 50 and 500, sometimes even more, different software solutions to operate their healthcare business, and in the United States alone the sector saw more than 900 hospital merger and acquisition transactions between 2014 and 2024, each of which brought their own systems, teams, and technical debt into somebody else’s landscape. So, there is an electronic medical record (EMR) system, or several of them after mergers, one or more revenue cycle management (RCM) systems for billing and patient accounting, a sometimes aging enterprise resource planning (ERP) system, a number of departmental specialty systems, research databases, and a long tail of point solutions, each of which brings its own data model, its own integration approach, and its own vendor release schedule. In such a landscape, every new capability becomes its own project, and every project has to solve integration, data quality, and compliance all over again, and the pace of change of the whole institution is effectively set by whichever vendor moves slowest.

The timing pressure is real, too. Many hospitals still run SAP Business Suite 7 (ECC), whose mainstream maintenance ends in 2027, with extended maintenance through 2030 and a Transition Option in SAP Private Cloud through 2033, and in Europe the secondary-use provisions of the European Health Data Space (EHDS) Regulation begin to apply in March 2029, which means the CIO’s delivery window for both obligations is essentially the same three to four years. And the platform market itself is being shaped in exactly that window: Gartner estimated in its April 2024 research "Innovation Insight: Digital Health Platforms Accelerate Transformation" that the digital health platform market was only around five percent penetrated, with adoption expected to play out over five to ten years, so the institutions making their architectural choices now are the ones whose requirements the market will form around.

The EHR-first instinct, and where it ends

Before laying out our answer, let me name the objection I hear most often, because it deserves a serious analysis. Many health systems reasonably prefer to look at their EMR vendor first for any new capability; successive Bain and KLAS surveys have found that roughly two out of three look first to their existing vendors, and especially their EMR vendor, before considering anyone else. I understand the instinct, since the EMR carries the clinical workflows and much of the clinical data, and nobody wants another vendor relationship without good reason. Yet the same market research paints a sobering picture of how that instinct works out in practice, with only about a quarter of executives in a Sage Growth survey saying their EMR vendor fully lives up to its promises. The deeper issue, though, is not vendor performance but scope: the decisions a CIO is now asked to support, from cost transparency per procedure to workforce planning to supply chain resilience to research data provisioning, depend on finance, procurement, workforce, and logistics data that does not live in the EMR and never will, and a system built to document and bill for care was simply never designed to be the enterprise data and AI foundation of a health system. This is, incidentally, also where the analyst community has landed: Gartner has addressed its digital health platform research explicitly to healthcare provider CIOs since its initial Market Guide in 2021, describing a shift away from monolithic, EMR-centric application suites towards a platform architecture, and in its most recent framing "Digital Health Platforms (Transitioning to Healthcare Provider Industry Cloud Platforms) Reviews and Ratings" positions the category as evolving into Healthcare Provider Industry Cloud Platforms whose purpose is to let a CIO adjust the application portfolio at the pace of business change. That last phrase describes the job better than I ever could.

Connect, govern, innovate, transform

Our strategy for the digital health platform powered by SAP addresses exactly this in four dimensions: connect, govern, innovate, and transform. Let me take them in the order an actual delivery program would.

To connect means to bring disparate systems into a single, coherent view across clinical and enterprise operations. The platform speaks the standards your landscape already uses, among them HL7 FHIR®, openEHR, IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise), OMOP (the common data model of the Observational Medical Outcomes Partnership), and, in Germany, ISiK as the national interoperability standard for hospital information systems. It connects EMR and revenue cycle management systems as well as EHDS-related sources, alongside SAP and non-SAP applications, whether they run as software as a service, cloud-native, hybrid, or on-premises. The AnyEMR/MultiEMR and AnyRCM/MultiRCM principles I described in the strategy post apply here, too: the platform integrates across whatever clinical and billing systems you have chosen rather than locking you into a single vendor’s stack, and since SAP does not build EMR or RCM systems itself, we unify across them together with and through our partner ecosystem.

To govern is, for the CIO, the most consequential of the four: in most institutions today, governance is a per-project exercise: every integration project defines its own access rules, every research request triggers its own anonymization effort, every audit finding produces its own remediation plan, and the sum of all this is a patchwork of controls that nobody can fully describe, let alone defend under time pressure. On the platform, governance becomes a managed capability instead. Master data governance, identity and access, consent and rights management, pseudonymization and anonymization, and data sovereignty compliance are built into the architecture and travel with every data product, so a rule defined once, for example who may see identifiable patient data and under which consent, is enforced wherever that data flows, whether into an operational dashboard, an AI model, or a research extract. That does not make audit readiness automatic, because audits will always also examine processes and people, but it makes audit readiness sustainable, since the technical controls no longer have to be reconstructed and re-documented for every new use of the data. And where residency requirements demand it, the same architecture can be deployed on sovereign-cloud options, so that the jurisdiction question is answered by design rather than negotiated per project. This is also where regulation stops being only a burden: an institution whose consent, provenance, and de-identification are managed capabilities enters the EHDS era prepared, while one that handles them project by project will spend those same years catching up.

To innovate then builds on that foundation rather than around it: a composition layer with analytics from descriptive to predictive, knowledge graphs for reasoning across clinical, operational, and financial domains, healthcare-specific semantics and ontologies, which are expanding to include terminologies such as SNOMED CT and the OMOP data models, and AI domain models including clinical natural language processing, all accessible through build tools from low-code to pro-code, so that extensions live safely outside a clean core and survive the next upgrade, a lesson many of us learned the hard way in the ERP world. And to transform is simply what follows when the first three are in place: decisions powered by governed data and embedded AI, at a pace the institution sets for itself.

The architecture, seen from the delivery side

At the top level, the DHP architecture consists of three layers, and increasingly a fourth. A data foundation provides clinical, enterprise, and ecosystem connectivity, including the healthcare interoperability standards mentioned above. A digital innovation layer carries the intelligence and the health-specific business context. A healthcare experiences layer offers applications such as patient portals, clinical workplaces, health apps and dashboards, and scenarios like clinical trial matching, patient identity resolution, and the EHDS health record. And on top of these sits the AI layer, in which embedded intelligence, assistants, and increasingly agentic capabilities operate on the governed layers beneath them rather than beside them. Readers who know Gartner’s published DHP reference architecture from their "Market Guide for Digital Health Platforms" will recognize the shape of the first three: an integrated data fabric with health data connectors, data curation and enrichment, metadata management, a healthcare-specific data model, and privacy and consent controls; a layer of packaged business capabilities; and a composition layer with low-code tooling for building tailored experiences. Our architecture is a concrete instantiation of exactly that pattern, and it deliberately extends it in the one dimension that has moved fastest since the pattern was first described: the AI layer on top. And for readers who want to explore how this platform category is developing across the wider market, Vince Kuraitis’s series “Platforming Healthcare – The Long View” offers a thorough long-term perspective.

What binds these layers together is the data product, and it comes in two kinds. Primary data products are sourced directly from a clinical or enterprise application and represent the original, unprocessed data: claims records from the revenue cycle management system, encounter summaries from the EMR, patient records from a clinical data repository (CDR), or financials from SAP Cloud ERP. Derived data products are curated and enriched on top of them, and here, health-specific content such as Patient 360, treatment cost, clinical workforce readiness, and patient risk scores, which deliver ready-to-use insight without additional integration effort, are being built out. Anyone who has run a hospital integration program knows why this matters: instead of maintaining n×m point-to-point integrations between sources and consumers, you build governed, reusable assets once and consume them everywhere, with lineage and policy enforced from the start.

This is what that AI layer changes in practice: because it is grounded in exactly this governed, contextualized data, and because it acts through the platform’s managed capabilities rather than around them, AI can be deployed where it demonstrably relieves pressure in daily operations. SAP Joule, our AI assistant, is being extended by SAP and partners with healthcare scenarios to address the everyday pain points of running a hospital, from clinical documentation, coding and DRG optimization, and denial management to medication replenishment, patient scheduling, regulatory reporting, and workforce planning, while differentiating industry scenarios around Patient 360, clinical workforce optimization, and denial prevention and revenue recovery build on the very same foundation. The CIO needs neither a separate AI stack nor a separate governance regime for it, and no separate integration program to feed it, because the platform itself is the AI foundation. It is also the path to what we at SAP call the Autonomous Enterprise: operations in which routine work increasingly runs itself, under the institution's own rules and controls. That kind of autonomy cannot be installed; it becomes possible only once connected data, business context, and governance are already in place, which is exactly what the layers beneath the AI provide.

That this approach works in practice is not a hypothesis: Hirslanden, Switzerland’s largest private hospital group, modernized its core processes and systems to support new clinical practices, regulatory standards, and cost reimbursement using SAP Signavio business transformation solutions, an example of how process intelligence grounded in a common platform accelerates transformation in healthcare rather than adding to the project load.

An ecosystem by design

No single vendor covers healthcare end to end, and we at SAP do not pretend to. Partners extend the platform with the clinical capabilities and industry solutions, built on SAP Business Technology Platform and SAP Business Data Cloud, delivered through SAP PartnerEdge, and available via SAP Store. For the CIO, that translates into freedom of choice at the edges and a stable, standards-based core in the middle.

A pragmatic sequence, not a big bang

Given the maintenance and regulatory horizons described above, a deliberate sequence is in fact the lower-risk path. It begins with mapping the landscape and agreeing on three to five cross-functional use cases that span clinical and operational domains. It continues with setting up the data foundation and publishing the first data products with governance in place, and with modernizing the ERP core at the institution’s own pace, be it with GROW with SAP for first-time adopters or with RISE with SAP for existing landscapes, where most providers favor separating the ERP and hospital information system transformations, with ERP first, to harmonize master data before clinical systems are subsequently integrated. It then connects the EMR, RCM, and priority partner solutions through standards-based integration including HL7 FHIR®, and it proves value early with time-boxed pilots before scaling responsibly.

The outcome is what the board asked for in the first place, and it is what running best means in healthcare: an institution that adopts innovation at its own speed, meets its regulatory obligations by design, and, where it chooses to, takes part in emerging health data collaborations on its own terms. I will not promise any CIO that a platform shrinks the portfolio overnight, because none does, and every practitioner knows that platforms are additive in the first place before they are consolidating. What it does provide is something more important: an operational model with a credible, governed path from a landscape that dictates its own terms to one that follows yours.

entry.title: SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 2 in review
entry.content:
In my Day 1 recap, I talked about the gap between AI investment and AI value, and closed with the ambition that the organizations that lead won't just automate what exists, they will reinvent it. Day 2 was where that ambition got concrete, showing what reinvention looks like in Finance, Customer Experience, and Supply Chain, and giving consultants a practical session on getting SAP Joule for Consultants running on live projects.

Here's my recap of the day's sessions and the questions that came up most.

AI is everywhere. Results aren't.

That was the opening line of the Day 2 Morning Briefing (recording), and it set the tone for everything that followed. 74% of organizations are stuck in pilot and the reason is structural: generic AI lacks business context, point solutions create patchwork, and building without governance slows everything down. Most enterprises are still assembling AI function by function, a copilot here, a point solution there. Each adds intelligence to a silo. None of them talk to each other.

SAP's answer is one system. The SAP Autonomous Suite is organized around five domains, Finance, Spend, Supply Chain, HR, and Customer Experience, where people direct, Assistants coordinate, and Agents execute. Domains move as one: a decision in Finance shows up in Supply Chain; a change in demand triggers a response in Procurement. Industry AI extends each domain with vertical knowledge and regulatory logic from day one. Explore all Joule Assistants here.

On Day 1, we looked at Joule Work as the unified workspace where this all comes together. Day 2 went deeper, showing what the Autonomous Enterprise means for Finance, CX, and Supply Chain specifically. The sessions that followed answered exactly that. Rewatch them:

  • From SAP Sapphire to What's Next in Finance: See how a billing process map changes when agents take over the work that shouldn't need a person (recording).
  • From SAP Sapphire to What's Next in Supply Chain Management: Get a sneak peek of the Asset and Service Assistant (recording).
  • From SAP Sapphire to What's Next in Customer Experience: See how CX becomes a revenue function by closing the disconnect between engagement data and business outcomes (recording).

Seeing what the Autonomous Enterprise looks like in each domain is the first step. Getting there is the next. The day's final session was built for the consultants and partners doing that work. Two features released at SAP Sapphire 2026 were the headline of this session, and both are already available:

Custom Knowledge Grounding connects SAP Joule for Consultants to your organization's own content such as project files, internal knowledge repositories, delivery standards, and policies indexed via SAP AI Core Grounding. The result: answers that draw on SAP's full knowledge base and your firm's institutional knowledge at the same time. Less time searching. Responses that reflect how your organization actually works.

  • SAP Joule for Consultants Custom Knowledge Grounding
  • Enhance SAP Joule for Consultants with Custom Knowledge Grounding

Expert Workspace enables persistent personalization in SAP Joule for Consultants by introducing reusable Experts with custom instructions and file-based grounding. It is a centralized environment within the SAP Joule for Consultants console where users can create, configure, and manage Experts at design time. Experts can then be explicitly activated during conversations at run time.

  • SAP Joule for Consultants Expert Workspace
  • Getting Started with SAP Joule for Consultants Expert Workspace

Rewatch From Activation to Application: Getting Started with SAP Joule for Consultants.

Before I wrap up, I wanted to address the questions that came up most:

  • What does it actually cost?
    SAP Joule for Consultants is a premium AI feature.     Pricing is user-based, reflecting scenarios where value is directly tied to individual productivity. It is offered as a Per User Per Month package, with a fixed number of 35 AI Units per user. Consumption is tracked in SAP for Me. Find out more in the Whitepaper Understanding the SAP Business AI Commercial Model.
  • What are the prerequisites for activating SAP Joule for Consultants?
    As a customer or partner, you will need to have AI Units and a SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) Global Account. There are no other technical dependencies and no system requirements. SAP Joule for Consultants is a standalone web-based assistant, which means on-premise, ECC, and private cloud customers can activate and use it just as easily as public cloud customers. Find out how to enable SAP Joule for Consultants in the Discovery Center Mission.

Day 1 set the foundation. Day 2 put it into practice. Continue with the full series:

  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 3 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 4 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 1 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 2 in review

entry.title: Building Custom AI Agents with SAP Joule Studio: A Practical Guide Part-1
entry.content:
Introduction

Joule is already available in SAP Launchpad for many users, and it is great for broad questions.
My challenge started when business users asked for operational actions, not guidance.

A user asked:
“Show all blocked sales orders for customer 17100001 and remove the delivery block for order 487.”

I am unable to directly retrieve or display information on open sales orders due to system limitations. However, I can guide you on how to use the 'Sales Order - Query' to access this information manually. Would you like me to provide instructions on how to do this?

Out of the box, Joule gave a helpful explanation, but it did not execute the business action.
So I built a custom agent in Joule Studio that could read and update sales orders in SAP S/4HANA through OData APIs.

The Two Building Blocks: Skills & Agents

Everything in Joule Studio revolves around two core concepts:

1. Skill — A Specific Task

A  skill is a single, focused capability. Think of it as one action your agent can perform. It could be:

  1. A CRUD operation (Create, Read, Update, Delete)
  2. A process or automation
  3. A  condition check
  4. Launching another Joule skill
  5. Each skill does one thing well. For example:
    • Read all sales orders
    • Update delivery block on a sales order
    • Create a new sales order
    • Delete sales order.
2. Agent — The Orchestrator

An agent is the brain that holds multiple skills together. It decides when to use which skill based on what the user asks. An agent can even call other agents — making it possible to build complex, multi-step workflows.

Anatomy of an Agent
When you create an agent in Joule Studio, you'll work with several important configuration areas:

Expertise & Instructions

  1. Expertise — Tell the agent what domain it specializes in. Is it for HR? Sales? Procurement? This helps the agent understand its boundaries.
  2. Instructions — This is where the magic happens. You're essentially writing a playbook for the agent:
    1. What to do and what not to do
    2. How to format responses
    3. When to trigger which skill
    4. Rules and guardrail

Additional Context — Common abbreviations, company-specific terminology, or any contextual information the agent might need to understand your business language.

Model Settings
You get to choose your LLM provider:

  • Anthropic
  • OpenAI
  • Google
  • MistralAI

Enable Backup LLM Provider. This sets up automatic failover — if your primary provider is slow or unavailable, the backup kicks in automatically, keeping your agent running without interruption.

MCP Servers
Add MCP (Model Context Protocol) Servers to give your agent access to additional tools and external capabilities.

Tools
Add tools that provide the agent with additional context, system data access, or the ability to perform actions.

  • calculator
  • joule skill
  • joule agent
  • Documents(Document Grounding)

Agent Output

Define how the output should be formatted:

  1. Markdown
  2. Text
  3. Data Type
  4. JSON
What I Built (Actual Implementation)

I implemented a Sales Order Assistant agent for a finance and sales operations scenario.

Scope of the agent:

  • Read sales orders with filters (customer, status, billing date, block flags)
  • Update delivery block on a specific sales order
  • Return a table-style summary for business users
  • Refuse to answer if live data cannot be read

Technical stack:

  • Joule Studio agent
  • 3 custom skills (GET, PATCH, POST)
  • SAP S/4HANA OData service: API_SALES_ORDER_SRV
  • Destination-based connectivity in test environment
  • Primary + backup LLM provider enabled
Step 1: Skills I Created

I separated skills by operation so tool selection remained predictable.

1. Read Sales Orders (GET)
Purpose: Retrieve sales orders and basic status fields.

2. Modify Sales Order (PATCH)
Purpose: Update delivery block and selected editable fields.

3. Create Sales Order (POST)
Purpose: Create a new order for approved scenarios.

Why this helped:
When I initially combined read and update into one “do everything” skill, the agent often chose the wrong operation. Splitting skills reduced tool-calling errors significantly.

Step 2: Agent Instructions I Actually Used

The single biggest improvement came from strict instructions.

Key rules I configured:

1. Always call Read Sales Orders before any status answer.
2. Call Modify Sales Order only when user intent is clearly an update.
3. Never generate order numbers, dates, or statuses without API output.
4. On 4XX/5XX, return a clear business-safe error message and next step.

Response format:

  • Always output a compact table for list queries.
  • Always include order ID, customer, billing date, and status.
  • Flag risky records: overdue billing, missing assignment, missing reference.

What changed after this:
Before strict rules, I saw occasional “plausible” but unverified responses. After adding mandatory tool-use rules, those stopped.

Step 3: Real Test Cases and Results

I tested using business-like prompts.

Test prompts:

  • “Show all blocked sales orders for customer 17100001.”
  • “Remove delivery block on order 500001234.”
  • “List overdue orders with missing assignment.”

Observed behavior:

  • Read queries: agent called GET skill, then summarized correctly.
  • Update queries: agent called PATCH skill with target order.
  • Failure case (invalid order): agent returned API error context and suggested verification steps.

One practical lesson:
If the API payload is too large, response quality drops. I reduced returned fields to only what users need and got more stable answers.

These were the most useful implementation learnings :

1. Keep skills narrow and explicit
Broad “multi-action” skills increase wrong tool selection.

2. Put tool-calling rules at the top of instructions
Priority placement improved consistency.

3. Add business-safe refusal behavior
If data retrieval fails, the agent must refuse to guess.

4. Minimize API response payload early
Too many fields create noisy reasoning and weaker summaries.

5. Add backup LLM provider, but validate output style
Failover worked, but I had to align formatting instructions across models.

6. Test with ambiguous prompts
Examples like “fix this order” forced me to add clarification logic before updates.

Execution Trace: What Happens Under the Hood (From My Test Runs)
For a prompt like “Show blocked orders for customer 17100001”:

1. Agent starts and loads instructions/context
2. Intent classified as data retrieval
3. GET skill called with filters
4. Raw response compressed
5. Agent applies formatting + risk flags
6. Final response returned as table

For an update prompt:

1. Agent detects action intent
2. Validates target order context via read
3. PATCH skill executes update
4. Agent confirms changed field and status

Real-World Example: Sales Order Assistant
Let me walk you through a practical example. My requirement was to build an agent that can:

  1. Handle natural language queries like *"Show all open orders for customer X"* or *"Remove delivery block on order Y"*
  2. Provide contextual insights (customer credit status, expected delivery dates)
  3. Suggest corrective actions and guide users through SAP Fiori apps

Step 1: Pick Your API

I went to the SAP Business Accelerator Hub and chose the standard OData service: `API_SALES_ORDER_SRV/`

Step 2: Create Skills

I built three separate skills — one for each operation:

Skill Operation Purpose
Read Sales OrdersGET Fetch all sales orders
Update Sales OrderPATCH Modify fields like delivery block
Create Sales OrderPOSTCreate a new sales order


The GET skill is that it retrieves all sales orders, but the agent processes the response based on your question. Ask for a specific customer? It filters and gives you only that data — not the entire list.

Step 3: Configure the Agent

Here's an example of how I set up the expertise and instructions:

Expertise:

You are an SAP Sales Order Assistant. Your expertise includes:
- Retrieving sales order details from SAP S/4HANA using OData services
- Explaining order status, delivery dates, and pricing information
- Helping users understand delivery blocks and billing workflows

Instructions:
CRITICAL RULES:
1. You MUST ALWAYS call the "Read Sales Order" skill before answering
ANY question about sales orders. NEVER answer from your own knowledge.
2. You MUST call "Modify Sales Order" skill when user tries to modify
a sales order.
3. If you cannot call the tool, inform the user that you need to
retrieve the data and cannot answer without it.

RESPONSIBILITIES:
- Retrieve sales order headers (order number, customer, billing date, status)
- Summarize order volumes by customer group, date range, or status
- Identify orders requiring attention (overdue billing, missing assignments)
- Present results in a clear table format

RESPONSE FORMAT:
| # | Sales Order | Customer Group | Billing Date | Status |
|---|------------|----------------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | 4500001234 | Group 1 | 2026-05-25 | Open |

DO NOT:
- Guess or fabricate order data
- Ignore Error 4XX responses — always inform the user

 

This is a basic example — in a real scenario, you'd provide far more detailed instructions based on your specific business requirements.

Additional Context

FIELD DEFINITIONS:
- Reference → Order reference / external document number
- Customer Group 1-4 → Classification groupings for the customer
- Assignment → Cost assignment or project reference
- Billing Date → Planned billing/invoicing date
- Exchng. Rate Accntg. → Exchange rate used for accounting
- Addit. Value Date → Additional value date for payment terms

SORTING & PRIORITY RULES:
- Default sort: Billing Date (earliest first)
- Highlight orders where Billing Date < today (overdue)
- Flag orders with empty Assignment or Reference fields

ERROR HANDLING:
- If API returns Error 4XX → inform user, suggest checking
authorization or filters

ORDER STATUS LOGIC:
- Billing Date < today + no invoice = "Overdue"
- Assignment field empty = "Needs Attention"
- Reference field empty = "Missing Reference"

Step 4: Connect Skills to Agent

The connection is straightforward:

Agent → Tools → Add Skill → Select your skill

Do this for each skill. Then, in your instructions, specify when the agent should trigger which skill.

Step 5: Test

Click Test, select your destination for the action calls, select your private environment, and start chatting:

  1. Give me sales order 987
  2. Show me all blocked sales orders
  3. Remove the delivery block on order 456

What Happens Under the Hood?

When you test your agent, you can see the internal execution trace. Here's what happens when a user asks something:

User Message

 

Breaking It Down:

PhaseWhat It Does
StartInitializes the agent, loads context, expertise, and instructions
Direct PromptingChecks if the request can be answered without complex reasoning
Decomposition Breaks the request into smaller sub-tasks
Plan Creates an execution plan — decides order of steps, which APIs to call
Agent Thinking The LLM reasons about the plan and decides the action to take
ReadExecutes the API call to S/4HANA
CompressorSummarizes the raw API response to stay within token limits
Agent Thinking (2nd)Processes compressed data and formulates the answer
Final Answer

Produces the user-facing response

Refinement

Quality check for accuracy, formatting, and completeness

End

Execution completes

Conclusion

The future of enterprise AI isn't just about having an AI assistant. It's about having your AI assistant, built for your business.

Joule gives strong baseline assistance, but business operations need domain-specific actions.
Joule Studio is where that becomes practical: you define the tools, the guardrails, and the behavior.

My key takeaway after implementation:
Good agent quality is less about which model and more about:

  • strict tool-calling instructions,
  • small and clean payloads,
  • and real-world test prompts with edge cases.

If you are building your first Joule Studio agent, start with one business process, 2-3 narrow skills, and strict non-hallucination rules. You will get better results faster than trying to build a broad agent on day one.

Remember: you're essentially teaching a new team member how to do their job. The clearer your instructions, the better the results. Once everything is working as expected — launch it, and let your custom agent work for your users.

entry.title: From pipelines to products- when your S/4HANA data becomes a product, not a pipeline.
entry.content:
What SAP actually changed: SAP becomes the producer

Data products are not new; the data-mesh community has argued for them since around 2019. What SAP did in Business Data Cloud (BDC) is structural: for SAP sources, SAP itself becomes the producer.

SAP-managed data products ship with the platform.
