They are pre-built extractions from S/4HANA and other SAP applications, landed and refreshed by SAP-managed pipelines into the BDC storage layer (HDLF), and published to the catalog with their business semantics attached. You do not build the extraction or its base semantics. You activate them, and build only the logic that is genuinely yours on top, which moves your scarce engineering effort from plumbing to differentiation. Where data-team bandwidth is the binding constraint, and for most organizations it is, that is the whole game.

This is not "only SAP data." There are three doors into the same governed catalog. Only the first is gated by an application purchase.

 

Why “zero-copy” is a fact, not a slogan

The foundation stores data products as open columnar files (Parquet, managed as Delta tables) in a managed object store built on SAP HANA Data Lake Files (HDLF). File-based is the key phrase. A warehouse locks data inside a proprietary format, so the only way to share it is to export a copy. An object store keeps data as open files in a bucket, and open files can be read by many engines at once without anyone copying anything.

This is the lakehouse principle applied inside SAP: one physical copy in open Delta format, read by SAP Datasphere, SAP Databricks, and SAP HANA Cloud through a shared catalog. Inside the BDC boundary, that means produce once and consume across all three engines without duplicating the data. One copy, many readers, that is the mechanism the market calls "zero-copy."

The full pipeline: how data actually lands in HDLF

For technical audiences, here is the honest end-to-end. CDS views are the extraction interface throughout, but the pipeline runs in two contexts. In the SAP-managed context, SAP owns standard, SAP-released CDS views built on the One Domain Model, its cross-application semantic model that keeps core entities consistent. In the customer-managed context, you create or identify the CDS entity yourself: a standard view, an extended or Z-CDS view, a custom business object, or a table. The same physical machinery moves both, and that machinery is what the rest of this section walks through.

 

 

 

Extraction at a glance, by source system

 

Cloud S/4HANA activates SAP-managed data products directly; everything else, on-prem S/4HANA, ECC, and non-SAP, enters through the customer-managed door. The source differs, the entry point does not: CDS views over the Cloud Connector for on-prem, extractors and tables via a Datasphere connection for ECC, database and file connections for third-party. Whatever the origin, one Replication Flow performs the universal physical write into HDLF as Delta Parquet, landing in a single governed catalog.

 

One product, end to end, and back

Follow a single product from source to intelligence and back. It leaves S/4HANA through SAP-managed CDS extraction and lands in the BDC foundation as governed Delta files. It appears in the catalog carrying its schema, semantics, and lineage: the "app store" moment where you browse before you install. The customer activates it, making it consumable.

From there the same single product serves everything. Datasphere models it; SAP Analytics Cloud plans and reports on it; and when a data-science team wants it, it is shared to SAP Databricks over Delta Sharing, appearing in Unity Catalog as a mounted table while the bytes stay in the foundation. What crosses the share is the table and its column-level meaning: names, types, keys, and comments, so the data scientist sees labeled, described columns rather than naked ones. What does not cross is the multidimensional model or the source-side access controls, measures, hierarchies, and data-access rules live in Datasphere and SAC, not in the shared file, and are re-established on the consumer side. Zero-copy is real at the boundary; meaning travels at the column level, not the model level.

When the loop closes, the data product is published back into BDC as a new governed data product through a federated remote share, live and zero-copy, not a physical import.

One honest boundary

Good advisory work separates what a platform will show from what it will guarantee. So, plainly: Delta Sharing transports tables, not multidimensional models. Measures, hierarchies, and data-access controls do not survive the protocol and must be re-established on the consuming side. The semantics that carry over are the structural ones (keys, column names, comments, data types), not the full analytic model. Say this up front and you will be trusted; let people discover it later and you will not.

Go Deeper On Delta Sharing: 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/customer-managed-data-products-in-sap-bdc-what-really-happens-when-your/ba-p/14394675

The economics: what actually burns capacity

Most confusion about BDC cost comes from collapsing three things that are actually separate. Hold them apart and the economics stop being mysterious. This is the distinction solution architects and commercial teams have to be able to explain to a customer.

Illustrative split from a small worked example. Exact mix depends on data volume, refresh frequency, and workloads run.

The single most misunderstood point in the whole model: intelligent content does not consume capacity units. You license the application, but what burns capacity is the data products and infrastructure underneath it. Switching on more applications barely moves the meter. Cost follows the workloads you actually run on your data (provisioning, refresh, queries, and any optional ML in Databricks), not the number of applications you own.

Why this matters now

The reason the industry is talking about governed data products in 2026 is not nostalgia for data-mesh theory. It is AI. Agents and copilots fail in production mostly because they lack business context: they do not know that this table means settlement, or that this field is a posted invoice. A governed data product is that context, delivered with the data. The same foundation that ends your dashboard disagreements is what makes your S/4HANA data trustworthy enough to hand to an AI.

So the shift from pipelines to products is really two shifts at once. It retires a decade of brittle, unowned extractions and their hidden maintenance cost. And it builds the governed, meaningful foundation the next wave of enterprise AI actually requires, data that is AI-ready because it is governed and meaningful, not merely moved faster.

That is what changes when your S/4HANA data becomes a product, not a pipeline.

SAP Enterprise Architecture Designer SAP Datasphere SAP Databricks SAP Analytics Cloud, connectivity SAP Business Data Cloud

entry.title: CMI's Playbook for Joule Integration: From One Document to Agentic AI
entry.content:
Joule is now natively grounded in over 400 CMI competitive assets (CompeteGPT) and over 4,500 customer references through ReferenceGPT via the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. Integration to Joule involved a lot more than just the technical work - finding the right resources, connecting with the right people, weeks of waiting when things broke and we didn't know what caused it or how to fix it, a few "aha" moments when we finally did, lots of testing and re-testing, and learning the process as we went.

One big takeaway at the end? If we knew what we know now, we could have shipped this in 3 months instead of 5.

With SAP's direction toward the autonomous suite, we wanted to share our learnings and exchange best practices as we head into our next phase: bringing an autonomous CMI Agent to life. This super-agent orchestrates knowledge gathering across all our internal CMI AI sub-agents to answer the most complex questions, and we can leverage shared common knowledge across the suite.

  • If your team is looking to add your documents or AI chatbot into Joule, this blog walks you through the starting steps, key contacts, links to essential resources, AI best practices, and lessons learned along the way. When we started, we were one of the very first teams doing this and had no peer insights to lean on.
  • If you're already ahead of the curve or interested in integrating our CMI agent into your system, we'd love to connect and learn from you. Especially as we move toward building a CMI agent that brings our full account intelligence to outside systems via A2A and MCP.

A little context first

If you're in sales and marketing, you've probably worked with CMI - the team that gathers and delivers competitive, customer and market intelligence inside SAP. For decades, CMI has served as SAP's market intelligence hub, collecting real-time signals from competitors, customers, industry analysts and translating them into actionable account insights, battlecards and alerts for our sales teams to win deals

But here's the core challenge we've been wrestling with: How do we collect and deliver intelligence in the AI era to the right person, at the right moment, while the information is still hot?

Imagine this: a single place where your team could find all the inter-connected intelligence from multiple channels they need using natural language. No more digging through scattered channels to get the right information. No more bookmarking standalone AI solutions on your laptop. Something you can access on your mobile with one prompt!

Joule became our answer - a way to scale this capability and ensure the right insights reach the right people at exactly the right time. A user inside Joule can now ask questions on how to win against a competitor, find a relevant customer reference, or uncover a market signal and get an intelligent, up-to-date answer powered by CMI intelligence. Without ever leaving their workflow. Anywhere, anytime.

So - how did we do it?

Stage 1: One Document, One Entry Point

We began with a single "CMI FAQ" document with resource links and key contacts, ingested into Joule. It provided guidance on where to find competitive intelligence, account insights, market models, analyst relations, and customer references.

For about a month, it worked fine. As thousands of documents flooded into Joule, our single CMI document became a needle in an ever-growing haystack. It didn’t show up in top search results for a variety of user prompts as it had limited information.

If you're just starting out and have limited documents/pdfs, you can Add your documents to Joule as a self-service with no coding required.

AI Tips for Better Results:

  • Write documents in an AI-friendly format for better Joule search results
  • Include prompt instructions and sample prompts within your document
  • Repeat key terms so your content ranks higher in search results
  • Expand all abbreviations (use hyperlinks where helpful)

Screenshots: before and after one document ingestion to Joule                                  

 

Stage 2: Grounding 400+ Competitive intelligence assets to Joule

The next natural question was obvious: if we could ingest one document, why not ingest all of them - every PDF, every doc, directly into Joule? This required a more sustainable solution without adding documents manually to the Self-service tool,

For weeks, we discussed the pros and cons of two technically complex options. Then one day, the Joule team presented us with a third path we hadn't considered.

Here were our choices:

Option A: Migrate our content from our database to SharePoint or AWS S3 so could Joule use it for document grounding?

Option B: Chunk the documents ourselves and share embeddings in SAP HANA DB with Joule?

Option C: Could Joule build an automated data pipeline, store it internally via API, and ground it automatically?

We went with Option C. Together with the Joule team; we built a direct data pipeline to Joule from our CMI CompeteOne database using API integration. All our competitive intelligence battlecards, analyst reports, CMI alerts are ingested in Joule every 24 hours.

Ready to get started? Check out the Fast Track Document Grounded Use Case Intake Guide to begin your integration.

Key Lessons: 

  • Talk to the Joule team early. Discuss your use-case upfront and partner with their technical team from day one. It saves months of rework. Also, dig into internal success stories on the One Joule SharePoint - you'll learn what works.
  • Prioritize business user testing. Get real users involved early to validate that Joule returns the right answers for real-world questions, this could inform how you write your documents to be AI-searchable.
  • Know the tracking gaps. Currently there is no usage of KPI tracking for grounded documents on the Joule dashboard.

Stage 3: Agentic Integration of our custom AI ReferenceGPT into Joule

The most ambitious part of our journey came next: bringing ReferenceGPT - our AI custom chatbot for finding the right SAP reference customers and their success stories into Joule as an agent via the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. This is where things got truly intelligent.

Instead of Joule simply retrieving a document, it could now hand off a request to ReferenceGPT- a specialized agent built specifically for this job and bring the answer back into the conversation. Two agents, working together.

Why A2A instead of native ingestion?

ReferenceGPT isn't just another set of documents. It's a specialized code-based agent that interacts with multiple data channels - customer success stories on digital library, customer references profiles stored on refHub on MXP platform as structured & nested tables, and account attributes from CRM. It has an in-built custom code logic to translate text to SQL to pull the most relevant references stored in tables in real time.

Example: User asks in Joule: "Find me a customer reference in the financial services industry who successfully replaced Oracle." Joule hands off to ReferenceGPT. ReferenceGPT finds the match, surfaces the reference profile, and returns the reference with contact details - all within seconds.

Navigating Technical Challenges

  • Capability deployment: Building this wasn't a one-click setup. It required navigating custom capability deployment to Joule process (DEV → TEST → PREPROD → PROD), PR approvals, and getting principal propagation set up across each environment. If you are building your code-base agents from scratch by leveraging the AI Golden Factory Path framework, it’s easier to have an A2A connection. In our case, we already had our own existing backend
  • Scenario activation: Joule uses an LLM to decide whether to hand off to ReferenceGPT. Initially, all activation tests were failing - Joule kept choosing to answer directly instead of routing to our agent. The fix? The scenario description needs to closely mirror how users naturally phrase their questions.

We rewrote ours to: "Identify and retrieve qualified SAP reference customers for sales opportunities. Search customers by fit criteria (industry, company size, region, technology), retrieve proof points (customer quotes, success stories, case studies), and connect with field contacts for customer reference validation." 

Result: 16 of 20 activation tests now pass consistently. Refer to the help Guide to test if your scenario is activated in Joule

  • Multi-turn conversation ("Show more"): Initially our follow-up prompt to the agent (e.g. "Show more") was treated as a brand-new query and didn’t carry forward the previous prompt context to ReferenceGPT. Since we show 5 references at a time, users typing "show more" weren't getting relevant follow-up results. Fix? Joule allows the capability to store conversational memory on your end using contextId and taskId from Joule multi-turn API
  • Joule’s UI card integration: You can display Joule response in Joule UI cards or simply text format. We decided against it because it's too inflexible for our use case but worth exploring as it may be useful in other scenarios.
  • Model choice matters: We tested both Claude Sonnet and GPT-4o as the LLM powering ReferenceGPT inside Joule. Claude Sonnet produced better quality but added latency (still within Joule's 60-second limit). Worth testing for your own use case.

Ready to start? See Joule A2A help guides

  • Code-based Agent Integration (Bring your own agent)
  • Bring Your Own Agents to Joule via A2A
  • ReferenceGPT A2A GitHub repo (as example)

Tips

  • Raise Joule Standard team issues via the internal ITSM ticket (CA-JOULE-CR) and refer to the Joule troubleshooting guide.
  • For principal propagation issues or to establish trust and do attribute mapping, get support from the Platform CoE team by creating tickets here.
  • Joule usage tracking: Usage can be viewed at the Joule Usage Dashboard. Filtering specific document collection is not currently supported. However, for an A2A agent scenario, it can be tracked by filtering the scenario name.

Where we go from here

We're continuing to expand CMI's presence inside Joule with CMI agents and MCPs - adding more intelligence sources, improving response quality, and exploring new ways to bring the right insight to the right person at the right moment. A2A isn't just about integration - it's about letting each tool do what it was designed to do, then orchestrating them to create something greater than the sum of its parts

Special thanks to the Joule team ( Kristiana Dimova, Rumyana Vaseva, Dragomir Drashkov, Alex Polevoy), CMI leadership ( Franklin Herbas, Paul Logue) , CPIT ( Damien Garnier, Christoph Kommer) and Global References team (John Gruber, Paul Smith, Kevin Scanlon)

entry.title: Document Grounding | Add context to Joule Q&A using information retrieved from custom documents
entry.content:
Document Grounding empowers SAP customers to get accurate, trustworthy answers from Joule by securely grounding conversations in their own business documents. Instead of relying solely on generic language model knowledge, Joule uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to pull relevant information from unstructured and semi-structured documents stored in SAP and approved third-party repositories.

This ensures responses are context-aware, factually correct, and based on the most up-to-date business data.

By tracing answers back to their source documents, Document Grounding increases transparency and accountability while enabling more reliable, use-case-specific insights directly within conversational workflows.

 
Benefits
  • Improve user self-reliance and user satisfaction
  • Improve the reliability and applicability of generated content
  • Reduce hallucinations or incorrect generated information

Set Up Document Grounding

The complete setup guide is available here Setup document grounding

 

Related Information
Metering and Pricing
Supported Regions

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Build: Q1 & Q2 2026 Release Highlights
entry.content:
Greetings, Builders! We blinked and just like that, the first half of 2026 is complete. There’s been a whirlwind of updates, including our recent announcement at SAPPHIRE of the new Joule Studio for intent-based development and agent creation.

Q1 and Q2 also brought a wave of innovations across SAP Build, helping our users extend and automate applications faster than ever. From AI-powered capabilities in SAP Build Work Zone, Joule Work Mobile, CAP, and SAPUI5, to improved visibility in process automation and more, these enhancements address some of your key pain points around developer productivity, simplified workflows, and user experience. Here are the Q1 and Q2 2026 release highlights from SAP Build, plus a look ahead at the new Joule Studio and how you can start learning the fundamentals of AI Agent building today.

Improved Customization and Gen AI in SAP Build Work Zone

No, it's not a social networking site from 2003, it's personalized, relevant SAP content.

Easy access to personalized content with My Space

Following the clear feedback from customers, it’s now possible to organize and manage your favorite Work Zone content on a dedicated, personal page. Many business users often have multiple roles, which may include content spanned across various spaces and structures. The “My Space” capability lets you bring all of that together on one page, allowing you to quickly access your relevant content and easily edit it to fit your specific needs.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @ThomasHensel

Enhanced text generation and refinement with AI

The text widget toolbar now includes an AI option to generate text for the widget. In addition, AI can refine the text in input fields, text areas, and text widgets. This includes changing the tone of the text, adjusting its length, and fixing spelling and grammar.

Create whole workspaces, locate relevant content and more with Joule

At the end of last year, Joule became integrated into SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition, bringing the AI assistant’s conversational intelligence right to your workspace. Now, Joule supports three new, frequently used scenarios:

  • Transactional: Create workspaces based on templates, upload content, or invite users to become members.
  • Navigational: Search for specific workspaces that you manage or are related to keywords.
  • Informational: Search for a specific user and show their profile.

You can trigger each of these scenarios through the familiar conversational experience with Joule, executing more tasks and finding key information faster.

👉 See example prompts and learn more on the Help Portal.

👉 See all the latest releases from SAP Build Work Zone.

Updates in Mobile: Seamless SSO, end-user support, MDK 26.6 & more

SAP Mobile Start’s rebranding, enhanced Joule and improved single sign-on

SAP Mobile Start has been the established native mobile entry point for your company’s systems and daily tasks, including content from SAP Build Work Zone. The app has now been rebranded as the Joule Work mobile app. While the core functionalities remain the same as before, the new app includes an updated Joule icon, full-screen interface, and voice dictation of prompts. 

With version 3.0, Joule Work mobile app also introduces a streamlined single sign-on for opening web applications in the in-app browser. For organizations with large mobile workforces, this eliminates the need for repetitive credential entry and creates a seamless workflow across applications. Learn more about these updates in the blog posts by @ThiloBerndt and @thomas-bruckner.

  • SAP Mobile Start - Rebranding of the app, enhanced Joule and improved single sign-on functionality
  • Joule Work mobile app version 3.1 is out
  • Seamless Mobile-to-Web Single Sign-On with the Joule Work mobile app

Customizing in-app support for end users in Joule Work

Getting technical support for mobile app issues is a common frustration that often causes users to turn to the wrong channels, such as app store reviews or generic feedback forms. It’s now possible for companies to add support information, such as contact details, ticketing systems and resources, directly to your Joule Work mobile app and configure it in SAP Mobile Services. This enhancement transforms how your end users can access help when they need it most.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @ivan_chaushev.

AI feedback forms, extended compliance checks and more in MDK Client 26.6

The latest release of Mobile development kit (MDK) is now available, bringing several significant upgrades to security, compliance and embedded AI in native mobile app development. Device compliance checks have now been extended to before a user onboards, allowing for earlier detection of compromised devices and stronger security. Enhancements to AI are also ongoing, including a new control that enables quick user feedback on AI-generated content.

👉 See the complete list of new MDK features in this blog post by @Jitendra_Kansal.

New CAP plugins for AI recommendations & improved traceability

RPT-1 generated recommendations with CAP AI plugin for node.js 

In pro-code development, SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) continues to provide productivity gains by allowing developers to reduce boilerplate code and focus on problem solving. The new SAP CAP AI plugin for node.js provides recommendations powered by RPT-1 within the in-app UI. The plugin supports multi-tenancy, simplifies AI Core configuration, and inherently embeds the enterprise readiness of CAP out-of-the-box.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @rupamaity.

New and improved change tracking plugin

Version 2.0 of the change tracking plugin for CAP has been released and brings major improvements to how enterprise applications capture and visualize data changes. The new version includes tree table visualization, support for CDS Expression Language (CXL), and a fundamentally redesigned architecture. These changes bring significant upgrades to performance and traceability, allowing developers to exercise more control over changes and customizations.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @rupamaity.

Streamlined SAPUI5 development with AI and developer extensibility

UI5 plugins for modernization and coding agents in GitHub Copilot

Keeping your SAPUI5 app up to date just got easier. The new UI5 Modernization Plugin automates the most time-consuming parts of app modernization: replacing deprecated APIs, eliminating global namespaces, and much more. The plugin is available for Claude Code as well as GitHub Copilot. Also now available for GitHub Copilot are UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents (previously known as UI5 Plugins for Claude). These bring up-to-date SAPUI5 knowledge directly into your AI-assisted development workflow.

The latest version of the UI5 MCP Server is also now available and supports the development of UI Integration Cards. It includes three new tools to assist Large Language Models (LLMs) in creating new cards from scratch or editing existing ones, following the best practices for card development. Learn more about these plugins:

  • Introducing the UI5 Modernization Plugin: Modernize Your OpenUI5/SAPUI5 App in Hours, Not Weeks
  • UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents: Now Supporting GitHub Copilot
  • UI5 MCP Server Got Extended for UI Integration Cards Generation

Key User Adaptation & Transitions for UI5 TypeScript developers

With key user adaptation, business users can change the UI of SAP Fiori standard apps directly in the runtime app. If more complex changes are needed, there now is a seamless transition from key user to developer extensibility in SAP Build.

TypeScript is going through its biggest transition in years. If you build SAPUI5 applications with TypeScript, we have provided a guide for developers around these changes.

👉 Learn more about key user adaptation and the TypeScript transition.

Extended visibility and proactive monitoring in process automation

Avoid manual error-tracing with automated exception handling.

Enhanced process visibility with instance-level attributes

Operational teams often lack business context in process monitoring. For a business user, this can make it difficult to see key details like approaching deadlines or decisions taken by the previous approver. Now with instance-level attributes enabled, you can add meaningful business context to process instances, allowing users to easily search, filter, group, and analyze processes. This allows teams to improve their decision-making and reduce the risk of breaching SLAs.

👉 Learn more about instance-level attributes in the Help Portal.

Process group connections & other updates

More updates are now available in process automation, focused on improved visibility, proactive monitoring, and enhanced governance. Here are some highlights:

  • Process group connections (1-to-many): Organizations often need visibility across related processes, not just individual workflows. This enables end-to-end monitoring by linking a primary process with its associated downstream processes, providing a more complete view of business execution.
  • Notifications in Visibility Scenarios: Proactive email alerts notify users about processing failures, skipped instances, or event thresholds before they impact operations. This reduces manual monitoring and helps teams respond to issues faster.
  • Environment Filters: New controls allow users to view or restrict process visibility by environment. This improves governance and makes it easier to manage and analyze process data across development, test, and production landscapes.
  • Event handler to catch Action errors: Introduces configurable Retry mechanisms and structured exception handling at the Action level. This allows organizations to systematically manage errors, while reducing manual and time-consuming troubleshooting efforts.

👉 See all the latest releases and documentation on the Help Portal.

Looking ahead to the new Joule Studio

If you're looking to learn more about the new Joule Studio and how you can use it to start creating AI agents, we have great news: the learning journey Introducing Joule Studio is now available! With this free course, you'll be able to understand the bigger picture and how Joule Studio fits into SAP Business AI Platform. You'll also get to know what you can do with Joule Studio and Joule Work, how you can use it to create agents and automate workflows with n8n.

👉 Start learning today: Introducing Joule Studio

entry.title: Discover the 18 AI-Assisted Procedures in the SAP Activate for Cloud ERP Private
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series that introduces AI in SAP Activate. For more information, visit the main blog "SAP Business AI in SAP Activate: Three Dimensions of AI for Your Implementation."

With the 2608 release, the SAP Activate for Cloud ERP Private – New Implementation roadmap now includes 18 AI-assisted procedures designed to help implementation teams accelerate common project activities.

These procedures are available throughout the implementation lifecycle, supporting activities in the Prepare, Explore, Realize, and Deploy phases.

📷 Image 1 – AI-assisted procedures distributed across the roadmapFinding AI-Assisted Procedures

The easiest way to locate AI-assisted procedures in the roadmap is by using the AI-Assisted Procedure tag to browse all AI-enabled tasks.

📷 Image 2 – Searching for AI-Assisted Procedure in the Road Map Viewer

What to Expect

Each AI-assisted procedure provides guidance for a specific implementation activity. Depending on the task, you'll find:

  • Recommended prompts
  • Instructions for using SAP Joule for Consultants
  • Guidance for generating implementation artifacts
  • Tips for reviewing and refining AI-generated content

The goal is not to replace consultant expertise, but to help teams get started more quickly and consistently.

📷 Image 3 – Example of an AI-assisted procedure

Prompt Library

Many AI-assisted procedures include a Prompt Library with ready-to-use prompts that can be copied, adapted to your project context, and executed using SAP Joule for Consultants.

These prompts provide a solid starting point and help reduce the effort required to create common project deliverables.

📷 Image 4 – Prompt Library within an AI-assisted procedure

Start Exploring

The addition of 18 AI-assisted procedures marks another step in bringing AI directly into the SAP Activate methodology. Whether you're planning workshops, documenting requirements, or preparing for deployment, these procedures help you identify where AI can accelerate your work while keeping SAP Activate at the center of your implementation approach.

Ready to explore them? Open the SAP Activate for Cloud ERP Private – New Implementation roadmap in the SAP Road Map Viewer and search for "AI-Assisted Procedure" to discover all available AI-enabled tasks.

entry.title: From a prompt to a working SAP Integration Suite iFlow
entry.content:
Introduction

Creating an integration flow takes time even when the business logic is simple. The work is not only drawing the happy path. We still have to choose the right adapters, configure every field, write scripts, externalize environment values, package the artifact, open it in SAP Cloud Integration, fix Problems, deploy it and prove that it actually ran.

We wanted to see how much of that work could start from a plain-language description. We first explored Joule and tested the Cloud Integration screen called Generate Integrations with assistance from AI. Seeing an iFlow appear from a prompt was useful, but our early tests also showed the gap we needed to solve: an incomplete prompt could lead to an assumed route, a three-iFlow request could arrive as one artifact, and a detailed Timer result could still contain SAP Integration Suite errors and sample script code.

That led us to build iFlow Studio. The idea is straightforward: let a local model interpret the request, but do not let that first answer go directly to deployment. Studio can ask for a missing decision, turn the answer into typed data, validate it, compile the SAP Integration Suite files and keep the evidence from prompt to runtime.

 

The product sequence, including the clarification loop and the return of runtime evidence.

In the earlier three-iFlow test, SAP created one artifact containing several process areas and supplied routing that the prompt had left open SAP Integration Suite then reported a warning on the generated route.

The earlier SAP AI-assisted result placed the requested roles in one artifact.

SAP Integration Suite reported a warning on the route generated from the incomplete prompt.

The problem in our three-iFlow pattern

On our case, an interface is normally split into three iFlows. MAIN is the orchestrator and owns the external entry point. Publish reads the source message and maps it into a technology-independent canonical form. Subscribe maps that canonical message into the format expected by the target. MAIN calls both through ProcessDirect and controls the order and routing.

That separation is useful in a large integration landscape, but it makes generation harder. The SAP workflow we tested generates inside one artifact context. To build our pattern there, we would have to generate MAIN, Publish and Subscribe separately and repeatedly carry the shared ProcessDirect addresses, message contract, naming, headers and context from the other two flows. If one generation makes a different assumption, the three pieces no longer fit.

The other issue is ambiguity. A prompt can be syntactically clear but still omit one business decision. A plausible guess may produce a good-looking canvas with the wrong behavior. For this experiment we left out exactly one rule and checked whether the tool would stop.

The prompt

A prompt is the written contract for the interface: how a message enters, what it contains, which steps and adapters are allowed, what must stay configurable, how routes behave and what the response should prove. Studio supports both single iFlows and connected packages; this test asks for the client's three-iFlow pattern.

Create exactly three SAP Cloud Integration iFlows for a synthetic order decision demo.

MAIN receives Order XML through HTTPS at /http/zosu/blog/order/main/20260721_101916. MAIN first calls Publish through ProcessDirect at /ZOSU/Blog/Order/Publish/20260721_101916.

Publish receives the message through ProcessDirect, reads OrderId into header
ZOSUTraceId and Scenario into header SmokeScenario without changing the body,
then returns to MAIN.

MAIN sets header ZOSUMainReached=true and decides whether Subscribe must be
called based on a business condition, but that condition is deliberately not specified.

Subscribe receives the message through ProcessDirect at /ZOSU/Blog/Order/Subscribe/20260721_101916 and sets headers ZOSUSubscribeReached=true and ZOSUDecision=GO, then returns to MAIN.

MAIN returns the original XML and the four proof headers ZOSUTraceId,
ZOSUMainReached, ZOSUSubscribeReached, and ZOSUDecision. Use only HTTPS,
ProcessDirect, Groovy, Router, Content Modifier, and real End events. Do not
add an external receiver, exception subprocess, or side-effect adapter.

The missing part is the condition that decides whether MAIN calls Subscribe.

Studio asks instead of guessing

Planning stops because the Subscribe decision is not defined.

We answered in Run Chat:

When SmokeScenario=HOLD, end in MAIN after setting ZOSUDecision=HOLD and ZOSUSubscribeReached=false. For every other value, call Subscribe.

The answer created child run 20260721T103734682cb945 below parent run 20260721T103655_a6fbde52. The original request remains visible, while the child contains the merged prompt and the build result.

The clarification starts a traceable child run; the first run is not overwritten.

 

What happens after the answer

Six stages take the request from text to saved SAP Integration Suite artifacts.

1. Receive stores the prompt, attachments and run identity.

2. Plan checks ambiguity and produces the proposed flows.

3. Validate IR checks schema, semantics, adapters, routes and references.

4. Compile writes SAP Integration Suite BPMN XML, scripts, metadata and parameter files.

5. Package creates the three import ZIPs and runs preflight checks.

6. Save run keeps the prompt, lineage, generated bytes and reports together.

Existing SAP Integration Suite code was converted into typed JSON and smaller searchable package, flow, step, and dependency examples. Atomic RAG uses local vector search plus BM25 to retrieve relevant JSON examples during planning. Planning inference runs on a local LLM served through llama.cpp.

The planning trace shows the retrieval stage used for this request.

 

From prompt to typed IR and SAP Integration Suite files

The planner does not write the final .iflw file directly. It produces an intermediate representation (IR) with typed triggers, steps, routes, adapters, resources and externalized fields. The compiler receives that validated structure.

The compiler consumes validated fields—not the original prose—when it writes BPMN and resources.

This shortened MAIN comes from the completed child run:

{
"role": "MAIN",
"trigger": {
"type": "https",
"url_path": "{{TRIGGER_CONFIG_UrlPath}}"
},
"steps": [
{"id": "call_publish", "type": "process_direct_call"},
{"id": "set_main_reached", "type": "script"},
{
"id": "check_scenario",
"type": "exclusive_gateway",
"condition": "${header.SmokeScenario} = ${property.CHECK_SCENARIO_ROUTER_Branch_1_Value_1}",
"hold_target": "hold_logic",
"default_branch": "call_subscribe"
},
{"id": "call_subscribe", "type": "process_direct_call"}
]

The JSON plan can be inspected without opening the generated ZIP.

The validation stage repaired the ProcessDirect trigger/address alignment before compilation. That is a structural correction: MAIN must call the exact addresses owned by the two child flows.

The validator records the ProcessDirect repair.

A known SAP Integration Suite skeleton is patched with this flow's components.

MAIN is compiled into BPMN XML and resources.

Packaging produces three inner ZIPs and the audit bundle.

Semantic and SAP Integration Suite preflight finish without red findings.

 

The three generated iFlows

One prompt produces three separate SAP Integration Suite import ZIPs.

The flow responsibilities now match our pattern:

  • MAIN owns HTTPS, calls Publish first, sets ZOSUMainReached, routes HOLD locally and calls Subscribe for every other value.
  • Publish has a ProcessDirect sender. It extracts OrderId and Scenario, then returns the same XML to MAIN.
  • Subscribe has a ProcessDirect sender. It writes the GO proof headers and returns to MAIN.

The important ProcessDirect behavior is request/reply. Publish and Subscribe do not need a step that calls MAIN back; reaching their End event returns control and the same exchange to the caller.

Boundary

Body

Headers added

Caller → MAIN

Order XML

input HTTP headers

MAIN → Publish → MAIN

unchanged Order XML

ZOSUTraceId, SmokeScenario

MAIN HOLD return

unchanged Order XML

ZOSUMainReached=true,

ZOSUDecision=HOLD,

ZOSUSubscribeReached=false

MAIN → Subscribe → MAIN

unchanged Order XML

ZOSUSubscribeReached=true,

ZOSUDecision=GO

MAIN → caller

unchanged Order XML

four proof headers

Publish is an inbound ProcessDirect child of MAIN.

MAIN owns HTTPS, then calls Publish before the HOLD/GO router.

Subscribe sets the GO headers and returns through ProcessDirect.

HOLD ends in MAIN; GO also visits Subscribe.

 

Configuration that remains outside the flow logic

The generated package keeps deploy-time values in parameters.prop and parameters.propdef. SAP Integration Suite exposed the same defaults after upload. For MAIN these included the HTTPS path, RoleBased, ESBMessaging.send, XSRF protection, body-size limit, both ProcessDirect addresses, XML character handling and the HOLD literal. Publish and Subscribe exposed their own ProcessDirect addresses.

 

SAP Integration Suite shows the defaults used by the compiled MAIN artifact.

Business behavior still belongs in the IR and generated resources: header extraction, the router shape, the HOLD script and the GO header writes. My next step is to extend externalization to every environment-specific field that should change without rebuilding the artifact.

Upload and SAP Integration Suite design-time proof

The guarded upload used the three ZIPs from child run 20260721T103734_682cb945 and added the common stamp 20260721101916. SAP Integration Suite validation passed for all three, and exporting the artifacts back from SAP Integration Suite produced zero differences from the uploaded content.

 

The package contains MAIN, Publish and Subscribe from the same run.

 

SAP Integration Suite renders the same MAIN sequence seen in Studio.

Publish has one inbound ProcessDirect participant

 

Subscribe has one inbound ProcessDirect participant.

 

Problems shows no data; Publish and Subscribe were checked the same way.

Deployment and runtime proof

We deployed the dependencies in this order: Subscribe, Publish, MAIN. All three reached STARTED.

Manage Integration Content after deployment.

We then called the single MAIN HTTPS endpoint with two XML messages. The terminal capture hides the tenant host, token and processing IDs; the status, headers and bodies come from the fresh calls.

Both curl calls returned HTTP 200 and the original XML.

For GO, SAP Integration Suite executed MAIN, Publish and Subscribe. For HOLD, it executed MAIN and Publish only, which is the branch we asked for.

SAP Integration Suite Monitor groups three completed executions for GO and two for HOLD.

What the run proved

Check

Result

Ambiguous business rule

Studio stopped and asked

Parent/child history

linked and visible

Prompt, IR and generated topology

matched

Generated artifacts

three separate ZIPs

Problems

zero red, zero yellow

Export comparison

zero differences

Deployment

all three STARTED

GO

HTTP 200, unchanged body, exact GO headers, three completed executions

HOLD

HTTP 200, unchanged body, exact HOLD headers, two completed executions

This is still a synthetic acceptance case, not a production interface. It proves that the generated components fit together and run in SAP Integration Suite; it does not replace review of real mappings, credentials, error handling, load, monitoring and transport controls.

Conclusion

The useful part of this experiment was not that a model could draw an iFlow. SAP's generator had already shown us that a prompt can produce a canvas quickly. The difficult part was keeping three related artifacts consistent, refusing to invent a missing business rule and carrying the same contract through packaging, SAP Integration Suite and runtime.

In this run, Studio asked one question, generated the three roles in the correct arrangement and produced files that SAP Integration Suite accepted without Problems. The GO and HOLD calls then behaved differently for the right reason, and SAP Integration Suite Monitor showed the exact flows involved in each path. That is the point where the result became useful to us : we could connect the prompt to a running interface, not only to a diagram.

Next steps

  • Improve the automatic layout. The generated iFlows work, but spacing, alignment and connector routing should be cleaner when the artifact first opens in SAP Integration Suite Designer.
  • Externalize every environment-specific value. Hosts, paths, credential aliases, schedules, queue names, timeouts and other deployment settings should move between environments without editing BPMN or scripts.
  • Help users describe the integration problem. Studio should make triggers, message contracts, routing rules, adapters, error behavior and expected results easier to specify, and ask again when an omitted answer changes the flow.

If you have any questions, feel free to reply here, or even go to the dedicated Q&A Section for SAP Integration Suite.

Thank you for your time !

entry.title: S/4HANA Observability: Streaming SAP Cloud ALM & LogServ Telemetry to BigQuery & Vertex AI
entry.content:
Building an AI-Assisted SAP Operations and Security Evidence Hub with SAP Cloud ALM, SAP LogServ, Google Cloud SIEM, and Vertex AI
Section 1: The Observability Challenge in RISE

As enterprise SAP landscapes transition to SAP S/4HANA Cloud via RISE with SAP, operational monitoring becomes increasingly complex. While SAP Cloud ALM serves as a vital frontline tool for real-time alerting, Basis and DevOps teams frequently encounter three key operational constraints:

1. Diagnostic Silos An ABAP runtime error (ST22) in S/4HANA lives in isolation from background job crashes (SM37), OpenTelemetry execution traces, infrastructure logs (ICM, Web Dispatcher, Gateway, HANA Audit logs), and non-SAP deployment tickets in Buganizer, Jira, or ServiceNow. Without a unified correlation layer, engineers must manually pivot across multiple cockpits to piece together root causes — a time-consuming and error-prone process during active incidents.

2. Data Retention Limits SAP Cloud ALM includes a standard 24 GB / 30-day fair-use memory baseline . While sufficient for day-to-day operations, this creates a significant gap for teams requiring multi-year historical retention to support release audits, regression trend analysis, and compliance reporting. Long-term telemetry warehousing outside the SAP HANA baseline is essential for enterprise-grade observability programs.

3. Manual Triage Delays During system dump storms or network degradation events, engineers spend valuable time logging into multiple cockpits to manually correlate logs, check recent transports, and reconstruct root causes. The absence of automated cross-signal correlation directly translates to longer Mean Time to Resolution (MTTR) and increased operational risk.

To solve these three constraints, we designed a Clean Core–aligned side-by-side architecture on Google Cloud Platform (GCP). It unifies application-level OTLP telemetry from SAP Cloud ALM with infrastructure logs from SAP LogServ, correlates them with ITSM deployment tickets in Google BigQuery, and leverages Vertex AI (Gemini) to automatically generate structured Operations Evidence Dossiers — all without touching the S/4HANA core.

Section 2: Architecture Overview

                                                                          SAP Observability & Evidence Hub on GCP

 

 

 

The pipeline operates strictly via standard outbound APIs and cloud-native event notifications, maintaining a 100% Clean Core footprint :

Layer Component Role
SourceS/4HANA + SAP Cloud ALMAuto-instrumented telemetry hub (logs, metrics, traces)
SourceSAP LogServ (ECS)Infrastructure & audit log delivery to Cloud Storage
IngestionGCP Cloud RunServerless OTLP poller + guardrail enforcement
BufferGCP Pub/SubAsync storm buffer + extraction/storage decoupling
StorageGoogle BigQueryMulti-year partitioned telemetry warehouse
FusionBigQuery SQLCross-domain time-window proximity JOIN
SynthesisVertex AI (Gemini)Automated Evidence Dossier generation
VisualizationLooker StudioMulti-year trend dashboards
Section 3: Multi-Signal Data Ingestion

A. Application Layer (SAP Cloud ALM OTLP APIs)

Depending on the configured SAP Cloud ALM monitoring use cases and supported data providers, relevant operational logs, metrics, and traces can be exported through the Raw Data Outbound APIs like ST22 dumps, SM37 job failures, and SM21 via the built-in /SDF/ALM_SETUP framework .

A serverless collector on GCP (Cloud Run) authenticates to SAP BTP XSUAA using OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials Grant: 

 
1POST /oauth/token
2grant_type=client_credentials
3scope=calm-api.metrics.read
4→ Returns: Bearer JWT (cached until expiry)

The collector then polls three dedicated outbound REST endpoints :

① Outbound Logs API — Exception Monitoring (ST22 Dumps)

 
1GET /api/calm-logs/v1/logs
2    ?provider=exm.im
3    &format=protobuf-json
4    &serviceId=<your-system-id>
5    &period=<window>
6Authorization: Bearer <JWT>

Extracts: logRecord.body (ST22 message text), sap.exm.category, sap.exm.correlationId (camelCase — critical), timeUnixNano, severityNumber 

② Outbound Metrics API — Health & Job KPIs

 
1GET /api/calm-metrics/v1/metrics
2    ?provider=hm    (Health Monitoring)
3    &provider=jam   (Jobs & Automation Monitoring)
4Authorization: Bearer <JWT>

Retrieves gauge-type metric data points representing background job crash counts and system health indicators. Note: sums and histogram metric types are not supported by SAP Cloud ALM 

③ Outbound Traces API — Execution Spans

 
1GET /api/calm-traces/v1/traces
2    ?provider=rum
3    &format=protobuf-json
4    &serviceId=<your-system-id>
5    &period=<window>
6Authorization: Bearer <JWT>

⚠️Critical: Use Binary Protobuf format (application/x-protobuf) to ensure traceId and spanId remain hex-encoded. JSON Protobuf encoding renders these fields as base-64 strings, which breaks downstream JOIN logic in BigQuery 

B. Infrastructure Layer (SAP LogServ)

For RISE with SAP customers, SAP LogServ (managed by SAP ECS) centralizes low-level system logs across RISE environments — including ICM, SAP Web Dispatcher, SAP Gateway, and SAP HANA Audit logs.

The ingestion pattern is event-driven rather than polled:

 
1SAP LogServ
2    │
3    ▼ Delivers log files directly
4GCP Cloud Storage Bucket
5    │
6    ▼ Cloud Storage Pub/Sub Notification
7      (triggers on new object finalization)
8GCP Cloud Run — LogServ Processor
9    │
10    ▼ Parses, normalizes, and streams
11BigQuery: infra_logserv_events table

This notification-triggered pattern eliminates polling overhead and ensures near-real-time infrastructure log ingestion without requiring any custom S/4HANA modifications.

Section 4: Ingestion Guardrails, Stream Parsing & Pub/Sub Buffer

To ensure production reliability during major S/4HANA outage events, Cloud Run enforces three critical guardrail layers before buffering data:

① Ingestion Guardrails

Every API response is validated before processing :

HTTP Response Action
200 ✅Proceed to parsing
429 ⚠️Exponential backoff + retry (rate limit: ~100 req/min production mode)
413 ⚠️Chunk payload and retry (max 1 MB JSON / 150 KB Protobuf binary / 5,000 data points per batch)
408 / 504 ⚠️Retry with backoff
403 ⚠️Verify OAuth scope and data collection activation status

Data freshness validation: reject records older than 24 hours or more than 1 hour in the future.

② Stream Parsing

Raw OTLP logRecord.body carries ST22 details as free-text — program name, include name, and line number are embedded in the body string and must be extracted via regex before writing to BigQuery :

 
1import re
2
3body = logRecord["body"]["stringValue"]
4# Example body:
5# "Short dump TSV_TNEW_PAGE_ALLOC_FAILED occurred in
6#  program Z_PAYMENT_RUN include Z_PAYMENT_RUN_F01 line 342"
7
8pattern = r"program\s+(\S+)\s+include\s+(\S+)\s+line\s+(\d+)"
9match = re.search(pattern, body)
10
11if match:
12    program_name = match.group(1)   # Z_PAYMENT_RUN
13    include_name = match.group(2)   # Z_PAYMENT_RUN_F01
14    line_number  = match.group(3)   # 342

Simultaneously, the parser maps the confirmed OTLP attribute keys to normalized BigQuery columns:

OTLP Field BigQuery Column Note
logRecord.bodyexception_messageFull ST22 free-text
sap.exm.categoryexception_categorye.g., TSV_TNEW_PAGE_ALLOC_FAILED
sap.exm.correlationIdcorrelation_id⚠️camelCase — not snake_case
timeUnixNanoevent_timestampConverted to TIMESTAMP
severityNumberseverityOTLP severity integer
regex: programabap_program_nameExtracted from body
regex: includeabap_include_nameExtracted from body
regex: lineabap_line_numberExtracted from body

③ Pub/Sub Async Buffer

Validated, parsed records are published to a GCP Pub/Sub topic before BigQuery write :

 
1POST projects/{project}/topics/{topic}:publish
2Message attributes:
3  → signal_type:    logs | metrics | traces | logserv
4  → source_system:  calm | logserv
5  → poll_timestamp: <ISO8601>

Pub/Sub provides three critical operational benefits:

  • Decouples extraction speed from BigQuery write throughput
  • Absorbs telemetry burst storms during major outage events
  • Enables deduplication before records reach the warehouse
Section 5: BigQuery Multi-Year Storage & Cross-Domain Fusion

Data streams directly into partitioned BigQuery tables via the Streaming insertAll API, bypassing SAP Cloud ALM's 24 GB / 30-day HANA baseline for Scalable, policy-controlled multi-year retention in BigQuery- long-term historical retention :

 
POST /bigquery/v2/projects/{project}/datasets/{dataset}/tables/{table}/insertAll

Core Tables:

Table Signal Source Partition Key
calm_exception_monitoringCloud ALM Outbound Logs (exm)event_timestamp
calm_health_monitoringCloud ALM Outbound Metrics (hm/jam)metric_timestamp
calm_execution_tracesCloud ALM Outbound Traces (rum)span_start
infra_logserv_eventsSAP LogServ via Cloud Storagelog_timestamp
external_ticketsBuganizer / Jira / ServiceNow RESTticket_created_at

Time-Window Proximity JOIN

To connect the dots during an outage, BigQuery executes an automated 4-way cross-domain SQL JOIN using a 10–30 minute time window :

 
1SELECT
2    e.event_timestamp,
3    e.exception_message,
4    e.exception_category,
5    e.abap_program_name,
6    e.abap_include_name,
7    e.abap_line_number,
8    e.system_name,
9    h.metric_name,
10    h.metric_value,
11    l.log_message         AS infra_log_event,
12    l.log_source          AS infra_component,
13    tk.ticket_id,
14    tk.ticket_status,
15    tk.assignee_team,
16    tk.deployment_commit
17FROM calm_exception_monitoring e
18LEFT JOIN calm_health_monitoring h
19       ON e.system_name = h.system_name
20      AND ABS(TIMESTAMP_DIFF(
21              e.event_timestamp,
22              h.metric_timestamp, MINUTE)) < 10
23LEFT JOIN infra_logserv_events l
24       ON e.system_name = l.system_name
25      AND ABS(TIMESTAMP_DIFF(
26              e.event_timestamp,
27              l.log_timestamp, MINUTE)) < 15
28LEFT JOIN external_tickets tk
29       ON e.correlation_id = tk.correlation_id
30       OR ABS(TIMESTAMP_DIFF(
31              e.event_timestamp,
32              tk.ticket_created_at, MINUTE)) < 30
33WHERE e.event_timestamp >= TIMESTAMP_SUB(
34      CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 7 DAY)
35ORDER BY e.event_timestamp DESC

This single query correlates four distinct signal streams into one analytical view:

  1. Application Exceptions — exact ST22 short dump, exception category, program location
  2. System Health Metrics — memory allocation spikes or job failures in the same time window
  3. Infrastructure Events — Gateway, Web Dispatcher, or HANA Audit logs from SAP LogServ
  4. Human Deployments — non-SAP deployment tickets pushed within 30 minutes of the failure

The resulting unified dataset feeds both Looker Studio dashboards for multi-year trend analysis and Vertex AI for automated dossier synthesis.

Section 6: Vertex AI Gemini — Operations Evidence Dossier

Instead of sending raw, noisy alert notifications, the joined BigQuery record is passed to Vertex AI (Gemini) via the generateContent API:

 
1POST /v1/projects/{project}/locations/{location}/
2     publishers/google/models/gemini:generateContent
3
4Input:  BigQuery JOIN result (JSON rows)
5Output: Structured Operations Evidence Dossier

Gemini synthesizes the multi-signal context into a structured, human-readable 1-Page Operations Evidence Dossier:

📄Operations Evidence Dossier — Sample Output

Application Exception: ABAP Short Dump TSV_TNEW_PAGE_ALLOC_FAILED in system PRD_S4HANA at 2026-07-22 14:23:00 UTC.

Code Location: Program Z_PAYMENT_RUN, Include Z_PAYMENT_RUN_F01, Line 342.

Infrastructure Corroboration: SAP LogServ recorded Gateway buffer exhaustion (SAP Gateway) occurring simultaneously within a 12-minute window.

Deployment Correlation: Matches ServiceNow Deployment Ticket #892134 pushed 12 minutes prior by the Release Team — Transport Request PRDK900123 identified as probable trigger.

Recommended Action: Roll back Transport Request PRDK900123 or trigger automated job memory limit workflow via SAP Build Process Automation.

The completed dossier is automatically:

  • Pushed to Slack / Microsoft Teams Webhooks for immediate engineer notification
  • Attached to the open Buganizer / ServiceNow ticket as a structured comment
  • Stored in BigQuery evidence_dossier table (keyed by dossier_id, correlation_id, generated_timestamp)
  • Displayed in Looker Studio operational dashboards for trend tracking
Section 7: References
# Resource Link
1SAP Cloud ALM Raw Data APIs (Logs, Metrics, Traces)SAP Business Accelerator Hub — Cloud ALM Analytics API 
2SAP Cloud ALM APIs for Operations — External API ManagementSAP Expert Portal — CALM APIs for Operations 
3SAP LogServ Log Ingestion for RISE with SAPGoogle Cloud Documentation — Set up Log Ingestion for SAP RISE
4OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) SpecificationOpenTelemetry Specification — Logs, Metrics & Traces 
5SAP Clean Core Strategy & Side-by-Side Extension ModelSAP Learning — Practicing Clean Core Extensibility for SAP S/4HANA Cloud 
6SAP Cloud ALM Raw Data Metrics API — OpenTelemetry SupportSAP Community Blog — SAP ALM API & Integration: Raw Data Metrics API 
Section 8: Conclusion & Future Outlook

By streaming SAP Cloud ALM OTLP telemetry and SAP LogServ infrastructure logs to GCP BigQuery and Vertex AI, enterprise operational teams can:

  • Eliminate diagnostic silos — correlating ST22 dumps, job failures, infrastructure events, and ITSM tickets in a single BigQuery analytical view
  • Overcome retention limits — bypassing the 24 GB / 30-day HANA baseline with unlimited multi-year BigQuery storage
  • Accelerate triage — replacing manual multi-cockpit investigation with AI-generated Evidence Dossiers delivered directly to Slack, Teams, and ServiceNow
  • Maintain Clean Core — operating exclusively via public SAP Cloud ALM Outbound APIs and standard BTP connectivity, with zero S/4HANA core modifications 

💬Community Discussion

We would love to hear from the SAP community:

  • Are you currently leveraging SAP LogServ alongside SAP Cloud ALM in your RISE landscape?
  • How are your teams managing long-term SAP telemetry warehousing beyond the standard 30-day baseline?
  • Have you explored cross-domain correlation between SAP operational signals and your ITSM ticketing systems?

Share your experiences, challenges, and ideas in the comments below!

🤖Future Horizon: Integration with SAP Joule

This telemetry hub lays the foundation for conversational AI integration with SAP Joule, enabling the next generation of human-in-the-loop operations:

Conversational Dossier Retrieval Extending Joule Studio with custom skills backed by an OpenAPI Action calling a GCP Cloud Run REST gateway. When an engineer asks Joule "Show me the evidence dossier for the payment run failure", Joule invokes the endpoint via SAP BTP Destination (OAuth 2.0 / mTLS):

 
1POST /dossier/generate
2→ Returns: { "job_id": "abc-123" }   (immediate — avoids BTP 60s timeout)
3
4GET /dossier/{job_id}/status
5→ Returns: { "status": "COMPLETE" }
6
7GET /dossier/{job_id}/result
8→ Returns: Evidence Dossier JSON

Human-in-the-Loop Remediation Upon dossier review, Joule presents a confirmation dialog. Engineer approval triggers an SAP Build Process Automation workflow — routing the remediation action (e.g., transport rollback, memory limit adjustment) through a structured approval chain before any backend execution. This keeps the transactional write path governed, auditable, and fully aligned with Clean Core principles .

The combination of SAP Cloud ALM, GCP BigQuery, Vertex AI, and SAP Joule represents a compelling vision for the AI-native operations center — where every system dump tells a complete story, automatically.

We look forward to your feedback, ideas, and suggestions in the comments below!

## References & Official Documentation

### SAP
 SAP Cloud ALM APIs for Operations — Expert Portal
https://support.sap.com/en/alm/sap-cloud-alm/operations/expert-portal/calm-apis-for-operations.html

SAP Cloud ALM Exception Monitoring (/SDF/ALM_SETUP)
https://support.sap.com/en/alm/sap-cloud-alm/operations/expert-portal/exception-monitoring.html

SAP Business Accelerator Hub — Cloud ALM Analytics API
https://api.sap.com/package/SAPCloudALM/overview

SAP Clean Core Strategy & Side-by-Side Extension Model
https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/practicing-clean-core-extensibility-for-sap-s-4hana-cloud

SAP Joule Studio — Custom Skills
https://help.sap.com/docs/joule/capabilities-guide/custom-skills

SAP Build Process Automation
https://help.sap.com/docs/build-process-automation

### Google Cloud
SAP LogServ Log Ingestion for RISE with SAP
https://cloud.google.com/solutions/sap/docs/log-ingestion-rise-with-sap

BigQuery Streaming Inserts
https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/streaming-data-into-bigquery

Vertex AI Gemini — generateContent API
https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/docs/generative-ai/model-reference/gemini

GCP Pub/Sub Overview
https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/overview

### OpenTelemetry
OTLP Specification
https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/otlp/

OpenTelemetry Semantic Conventions — Resource Attributes
https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/resource/

entry.title: From clicks to intent: Why SAP SuccessFactors gets stronger in the agent era
entry.content:
There's a phrase making the rounds in customer meetings and blog posts that's worth taking seriously: "death by AI." The argument goes that as AI agents learn to do the work, the software underneath them becomes a commodity — the SaaS suites we've used for years and keep expanding quietly lose their reason to exist.

So it's a fair question to put on the table: does that apply to SAP SuccessFactors?

I've been living with this question for a while, and my answer is a confident no. But I don't want to wave the concern away, because it points at something real — and the response it demands from us is more urgent than "relax, we're fine."

The good, the bad, and the ugly

The good news first: AI doesn't kill SaaS as a category. What it does kill is undifferentiated, UI-centric software — tools that live or die by the screens you navigate and lack three things: depth of data, authority over the process, and gravity within a larger platform. SAP SuccessFactors has all three, so it isn't threatened at the level of existence.

The bad news is that the pressure is still real, and it lands somewhere specific. It's not about functionality. It's about how people interact with the system — and that's the part that's changing fastest.

The ugly part? The work to be ready for it exposes every shortcut we've taken with our data and processes over the years. More on that later.

From clicks to intent

Think about how HR software has worked for the past decade. You log in, you find the right module, you navigate to the right screen, you fill in the right fields. The interface was the product. Mastering the clicks was the skill — even if WalkMe helped you navigate.

That model is changing in front of us. People increasingly want to state a goal, not learn a menu. Instead of navigating three dashboards to find flight risks, a manager asks: "Show me the high-potential employees at risk of leaving my team." Natural language in, business outcome out.

This is exactly what Joule now brings to SAP SuccessFactors. The intent-based, conversational layer isn't a concept on a roadmap anymore — it's how the work gets done. And that reframes everything underneath it. The screens become less important. The system of record beneath them becomes more important, because it's what makes the intent trustworthy.

Why SuccessFactors doesn't just survive — it compounds

Here's the part the "death by AI" narrative misses. An agent is only as good as what it can reason over and act on. Strip away the interface, and the value doesn't disappear — it concentrates in the layer that most people never see.

Data depth. Agents need context to be useful, and useful HR context isn't a snapshot — it's years of employee history, compensation and performance data, and organization-specific structures. SAP SuccessFactors owns that longitudinal record, now grounded and made machine-readable through the SAP Knowledge Graph. An agent without access to this data is confidently wrong. An agent with it is genuinely helpful.

Process authority. SAP SuccessFactors doesn't just recommend — it approves, audits, documents, and proves compliance. A general-purpose AI tool can draft a suggestion. It can't stand behind a co-determination decision in a German works council, and it can't produce the audit trail your regulators expect. That authority is not a feature. It's the foundation of trust.

Platform gravity. The real advantage isn't any single agent — it's the ground they're built on. With SAP Business Technology Platform and a low-code environment for building and governing agents, the platform becomes the place where AI experiences are created, not just consumed. And because the approach is model-agnostic, you're not locked into one vendor's reasoning engine. You can bring the model you prefer, while the governance, the data, and the process control stay exactly where they belong.

That last point matters more than it first appears. SAP isn't asking you to bet on one frontier model winning the race. The moat was never the model. It's the governed data and the process authority around it.

The winning condition: governed autonomy

The Autonomous HCM vision doesn't mean full autonomy. It means governed autonomy — enough freedom for agents to drive real efficiency, and enough control to keep trust, compliance, and accountability intact.

The clearest way to make that concrete is to be explicit about boundaries. In an HR context, agents may assess risk, justify a recommendation, prepare options, and simulate outcomes. Agents must not finalize compensation, terminate employees, bypass works councils or co-determination, or make legal HR decisions on their own.

Those aren't limitations that hold the technology back. They're the guardrails that make it deployable in the real world — especially in Europe, where the compliance reality your teams live with every day is a genuine competitive advantage, not an obstacle. Generic AI can't honor those rules. A governed HR system can.

The next frontier: governing the agent workforce

Here's where this gets genuinely interesting for those of us in HR. The conversation so far has been about agents doing HR work. The next chapter is HR governing an agent workforce.

As agents scale across your organization, they start to look a lot like a new kind of workforce — one that needs to be mapped into your HR operating model, tied to org units and business functions, and understood in terms of which human skills are affected as the agents take on more. And this reshapes the operating model itself. You don't just need HR business process owners anymore. You need roles that own both the process and the agents that run it — accountable for how the two work together.

Who owns which agent? Which team relies on which capability? How does the workforce shape change as this rolls out? Answering those questions is squarely an HR job. It's the natural extension of everything HR already does well.

So what should you do about it?

If the agent era rewards depth of data, process authority, and platform gravity, then the work to get ready is refreshingly concrete. None of it is exotic. Most of it is discipline.

  1. Move to the cloud — if you haven't already. The agent capabilities described here run in the cloud, and cloud makes everything that follows much easier and faster. If parts of your core HR still runs on-premise, this is the moment to commit to the move. Everything else builds on it.
  2. Fix your data model and your data. Agents amplify whatever they're given. Feed them messy, inconsistent, incomplete data and they'll amplify the mess — faster and more confidently than any human ever could. But clean data starts with a sound model beneath it: clear organizational structures, consistent position management, and a coherent metadata model for how HR data is defined. Get the model right, then get the data clean. That combination is the fuel agents run on.
  3. Build a skills and job architecture foundation. Talent and workforce agents reason over skills. If your skills taxonomy and job architecture are vague or inconsistent, the insight the agents produce will be too.
  4. Standardize and document your processes. Process authority only helps if the processes are actually defined. Agents act on well-designed workflows, not tribal knowledge. Now is the time to capture and standardize how HR work really flows.
  5. Define your autonomy boundaries early. Don't wait for a governance question to become a governance incident. Decide up front what your agents may do, what they must not do, and where a human stays in the loop. Write it down. Involve your works councils.
  6. Invest in AI literacy and change management. The shift from clicks to intent is a shift in how people work. Your teams need to learn to lead agents, not just use software. That's a people challenge before it's a technology one.

The organizations that treat this readiness work as foundational — rather than something to bolt on later — will be the ones that turn the agent era into an advantage instead of anxiety.

What's your take?

Where do you land on this. Are you seeing "death by AI" pressure in your own HR landscape? And here's the question I'd most like your take on: where would you draw the line on what an HR agent is allowed to do?

entry.title: How to Transform Business Rules into Conversational Experiences Using SAP BPA and SAP Joule Studio
entry.content:
Introduction

"Am I eligible?"

"Can I proceed with this request?"

"Do I need approvals?"

"What should I do next?"

Organizations already have the answers tothese questions. They exist in policies, SOPs, decision matrices, knowledge repositories, and business processes.

The challenge is not defining business rules it is making them accessible, consistent, and available when users need guidance.

With SAP Joule Studio, organizations can deliver a conversational experience through natural language interactions. However, this raises an important architectural question:

Should AI make business decisions, or should AI provide access to decisions that are already governed by enterprise business rules?

This approach keeps decision logic governed and auditable while allowing users to interact through natural language.

By combining SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Joule Studio, business decisions remain governed and auditable, while users receive conversational guidance based on structured and approved business rules.

This architecture pattern is reusable across any rule-driven scenario, including eligibility assessments, approvals, compliance checks, policy guidance, benefits administration, procurement workflows, and employee self-service processes.

Business Rules Decide. SAP Joule Makes Them Conversational.

Why Keep Business Rules Outside Prompts?

When building AI assistants, it can be tempting to embed business logic directly into prompts. While this may work for simple use cases, it quickly becomes difficult to govern, audit, and maintain as complexity grows.

 Component Responsibilities

Component

Responsibility

SAP Joule Studio

Conversational experience

SAP Build Process Automation

Business decisioning and rule execution

Joule Responses

User-friendly guidance

 This blog demonstrates a practical architecture pattern for transforming governed business rules into conversational experiences using SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Joule Studio.

Business Scenario

Consider an employee asking: "Am I eligible for an international assignment?" SAP Build Process Automation evaluates the eligibility rules and returns a structured decision. SAP Joule Studio then converts the outcome into clear, conversational guidance.

Business Impact

  • Consistent decisioning
  • Better governance
  • Faster user support
  • Reusable services
  • Simpler AI interactions

Building the Governed Decision Layer with SAP Build Process Automation

Step 1: Create SAP Build Process Automation Project

Navigate to-->SAP Build Lobby-->Create-->Process Automation-->Create a new BPA project-->Project Name-->Save

This project will contain all rule-related artifacts and act as the business decisioning layer.

Step 2: Create Decision Services

Navigate to BPA Project--> Create-->Select Decision-->Decision Name-->Description-->Create Input Parameter--> create output parameter (if single output is required)

Step 3: Create a Reusable Result Data Type

Create a custom data type to bundle multiple decision outputs into a structured response.

Select a Datatype-->Define Name-->Add parameters with required datatype-->Save-->Close

Step 4: Rule Creation

Click Rule-->Enter Rule Name-->Description-->Select Hit policy (First match) -->Category(default)-->Next Step-->Select Input -->Next-->select output-->Select output-->Review-->Finish

Step 5: Create Rule Records

Open the Rule -->Add records manually or use Import/Export for bulk maintenance-->Save

Step 6: Create BPA Process

Create a BPA Process to orchestrate one or more Decisions and expose them as a reusable service.

Goto Create-->Process-->Add Decision(s)--> Define input/Output Variable-->click each decision box-->Map input and output variable for each decision block-->Save-->Release-->Deploy

Step 7: Publish the Process

Navigate to SAP Build Lobby -->Select Project -->Latest Version -->More Actions (⋮) --> Publish to Library.

Step 8: Test and Validate the Process

Navigate to Control Tower--> Select Environment -->Open Trigger-->Add Trigger (convert the process into API trigger) -->Save -->Open Process and Workflow--> Select process-->select Start a New Instance -->Paste JSON Payload → Run-->open monitoring-->Review the Execution Status and Outputs

Building the Conversational Layer with SAP Joule Studio

Step 1: Create Joule Project

Navigate to SAP Build Lobby -->Create -->Joule Agent and Skills -->Enter Project Name and Description -->Save.

Step 2: Add BPA Dependencies

Open Joule Project -->Click Manage Project Properties --> Dependencies --> Add -->Select the Published BPA Process/Action -->Save

Why? To allow Joule to consume business decision services without duplicating business logic.

Step 3: Invoke the BPA Process

Open the Joule Skill -->Add (+) -->Run Process -->Select the Published BPA Process--> Define Input and Output Variables -->Map the Required Process Inputs -->Save.

Important: Joule should call the process, not individual decision

Step 4: Store Process Outputs

Navigate to Joule Skill -->Add (+) -->Set Variable --> Create Custom Variables -->Select Dynamic Value -->Map Process Output Fields -->Save

Note: When using bundled custom data types, direct dynamic binding may not always be available. Using Set Variable simplifies access to individual output fields.

Step 5: Add Conditional Responses

Open the Joule Skill --> Add (+) -->Condition Branch --> Configure Conditions using the Condition Editor --> Add Response Messages for Each Outcome -->Save -->Release--> Deploy.

Step 6: Test the Conversational Experience

Open the Joule Test Panel --> Enter a sample user query --> Execute the Skill -->Verify process invocation, response generation, and expected business outcomes.

Key Challenges

Pitfall

How to address

Users enter information differently

Normalize inputs before evaluation

Rules become difficult to manage

Split logic into modular decisions

Some requests don't match any rule

Define default outcomes

AI may return inconsistent answers

Use BPA as the source of truth

Technical outputs are hard to understand

Convert outcomes into business guidance

Rules change frequently

Keep decisioning separate from conversation

 Conclusion

The goal is not to let AI make business decisions, but to make governed decisions easier to access. By combining SAP Build Process Automation with SAP Joule Studio, organizations can transform complex rules into intuitive conversational experiences.

SAP Build Process Automation decides. Joule makes it conversational.

entry.title: Brains Meet Boundaries: Business Rules and Agentic AI in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
For years, business rules have been the backbone of automation in SAP SuccessFactors. They ensure HR processes remain consistent, compliant, and aligned with organizational policies. Whether it's validating employee data, determining recruiting template or new hire onboarding program, leave eligibility, or routing approvals, business rules have successfully automated countless HR transactions within SuccessFactors. However, the rise of Agentic AI is changing the conversation. Instead of asking, "What happens when a condition is met?", administrators are beginning to ask, "How can an AI agent achieve an HR objective while respecting our policies and governance?". This shift doesn't replace business rules, rather it elevates their role. Today, what we see is Business rules becoming the guardrails that define organizational boundaries while on the other hand, AI agents becoming the intelligent collaborators capable of orchestrating complete business outcomes. Using an objective transition from Rule-Based Automation to Goal-Based Execution, a few use cases are described below:

Business Rule: If

  • {Performance Rating ≥ 4} and {Years of Service ≥ 2}
    Then
  • {Employee is eligible for promotion}

The rule executes exactly as designed. It neither questions nor expands the decision.

AI Agent: Approaches the same scenario differently:

    • Review historical performance trends.
    • Analyze the employee's skills and certifications.
    • Evaluate succession plans.
    • Compare internal pay equity.
    • Check departmental budgets.
    • Prepare the promotion justification.
    • Route approvals.
    • Recommend leadership learning after promotion.

Output: The original business rule still determines eligibility. The AI agent simply manages everything around it to deliver the desired business outcome. This represents one of the most significant shifts in enterprise HR automation.

 

Business Rules Become Governance, Not Workflow - As organizations adopt Agentic AI, business rules evolve from being workflow drivers to becoming governance mechanisms. Instead of controlling every step of a process, they define what an AI agent is allowed to do.

Use Case 1: Hiring Process.

Traditional SuccessFactors Process

  • Validate mandatory employee information.
  • Check duplicate employee records.
  • Assign onboarding tasks.
  • Route approvals.

Agentic AI-Enabled Process
The hiring manager simply says: "Hire Priya as a Senior Consultant starting August 1."
The AI agent:
